Abu Qays bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Hijaz, Yamamah; al-Harith bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; al-Hubab bin Jaz' — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; al-Nu'man bin 'Asar — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; al-Sa'ib bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Fihl; al-Saib bin al-'Awwam bin Khuwaylid — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Makkah / Hijaz, Yamamah; al-Taufayl bin 'Amr al-Dausi — Companion of the Prophet (Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Nabi)) in Yemen / Hijaz, Yamama; Amir bin al-Bukayr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medina, Yamamah; Amr ibn Sa'id ibn al-'Aas — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; Asma bin Ri'ab bin Hunayf — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; Atik b. Qays b. Basheer bin 'Atik — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; Bashir ibn Sa'd — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Syria, Aynal-Tamr; Habbar bin Sufyan — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; Habib bin 'Amr bin Mihsan — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Yamama; Harith bin Abi Sa'sa'a — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamamah; Harith bin Qays bin Khalid — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; Hisham ibn al-'Aas — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah, Ajanadin; Jabir bin Abi Sa'sa'a — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Hijaz, Mau'ta; Ma'n bin 'Adiy bin al-Jadd — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina / Hijaz, Yamama; Malik bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Hijaz, Yamamah; Qays bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Yamama; Sa'id bin 'Amr al-Tamimi — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; Sa'id bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; Shuja bin Wahb al-Asadi — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Hijaz, Yamamah; Tamim/Bishr bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Ajanadin; Thabit bin Aqram — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina / Hijaz; Thabit bin Hazzal — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamamah; Tulayb bin 'Umayr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Ajnadin, Syria; Ukkasha bin Mihsan al-Asadi — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medina; Umara bin Hazm ibn Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamama; Umayr bin Ri'ab bin Hudhayfa — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Aynal-Tamr; Umm Qays bint Mihsan al-Asadi — Female Companion (Female Narrator (Rawyya) of Hadith) in Makkah / Medina; Uqba bin 'Amir bin Nabi — Companion of the Prophet (From the First Pledge of Aqabah) in Medinah / Hijaz, Yamamah; Wazfah/Waraqa bin Iyas b 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Medina, Yamamah; Yazid bin Ruqaysh al-Asadi — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Hijaz, Yamamah.

figures.name: Abdur Rahman bin Qayzi bin Qays
Abu Dujana Simak bin Aus bin Kharasha
Attab bin Usayd bin Abi al-'Is

title: Death of Abu Bakr — Umar becomes Caliph
description: Abu Bakr dies after two years as Caliph and is succeeded by Umar ibn al-Khattab who will preside over the most dramatic territorial expansion in Islamic history.
outcome: Umar inherited a unified Arabia and armies already advancing into two empires

title: Battle of Ajnadayn
description: Muslim forces win a decisive victory over a large Byzantine army at Ajnadayn in Palestine opening the way for the conquest of greater Syria.

figures.name: Khalid ibn al-Walid
figures.role: field commander; led the decisive engagement

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: commanded one wing of the Muslim army

location.modern: Between Ramla and Beit Guvrin, Palestine

outcome: Opened Palestine and Syria to Muslim conquest; Byzantine field army shattered

visual.caption: Ajnadayn
visual.modern: Between Ramla and Beit Guvrin, Palestine

title: Deaths in 635 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 635 CE: Abdullah bin Qayzi bin Qays — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Jasr; Ansa — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Abyssinia / Medinah; Basheer bin 'Unbus bin Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Syria, Jasr; Khalid bin Sa`id ibn al-'As — Companion of the Prophet (Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation) in Makkah / Syria, Marj Safar; Qayzi bin Qays — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Ajnadin; Salama bin Hisham — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Murj Safar; Saleet bin Saleet bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam) in Abyssinia / Hijaz, Yamamah; Thabit bin al-Nu'man bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Syria, Jasr; Uqba bin Qayzi bin Qays — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Jasr; Waqid bin 'Abdullah — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah.

title: Deaths in 636 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 636 CE: Abdullah bin Mirba' bin Qayzi — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Jasr; Abdullah bin Sa'sa'a — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Iran, Jasr; Abdullah bin Sufyan — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Yarmouk; Abdur Rahman bin Mirba' bin Qayzi — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Hijaz, Jasr; Abu al-Rome ibn 'Umair — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Yarmouk; Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet) in Makkah / Medina; al-Mundhir bin Qays bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Iran, Jasr; Amr bin 'Uthman bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah, Qadisia; Ayyash bin Abi Rabi'a — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria; Firas bin al-Nadr b al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Syria, Yarmouk; Nawfal ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet) in Makkah / Medinah; Qays bin al-Sakan bin Qays — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Iran, Jasr; Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet) in Makkah / Medinah; Sa'd bin Khalifa — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Qadisiya, Iraq, Qadisiya; Salit bin Qays bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Iran, Jasr; Salma bin Aslam — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina / Iran, Jasr; Salma bint Aslam — Female Companion (Ansari Companion (Sahabiyya) of Madinah) in Medina; Suwayd bin al-Nu'man — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medina / Iran, Qadsia; Zayd bin Milhan — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Iran, Jasr.

figures.name: Abdur Rahman bin Mirba' bin Qayzi

title: Battle of Yarmouk
description: A outnumbered Muslim army crushes the Byzantine forces at the Yarmouk River ending six centuries of Roman rule over the Levant in a single day.

figures.name: Khalid ibn al-Walid
figures.role: field commander; executed the decisive flanking manoeuvre

figures.name: Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah
figures.role: overall commander of Muslim forces in Syria

sources: Hugh Kennedy, The Great Arab Conquests
outcome: Permanent end of Byzantine rule in the Levant; Syria, Palestine, Lebanon passed to Muslim control

title: Battle of Qadisiyyah
description: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas leads the Muslims to a decisive victory over the Sassanid Persian army at Qadisiyyah opening Iraq to Muslim control.

figures.name: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas
figures.role: commander; led Muslims to victory over Sassanid army

outcome: Opened Iraq and the entire Sassanid heartland to Muslim conquest

title: Deaths in 637 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 637 CE: Abu Akhzam/Ahzam bin 'Atik ( , — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medina / Iran, Jasr; Sa'd ibn Ubadah — Companion of the Prophet (Sayyid al-Ansar — Chief of the Helpers) in Medina / Syria; Tha'laba bin 'Amr bin Mihsan — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina / Iran, Jasr.

title: Conquest of Jerusalem (Ilya)
description: Caliph Umar personally travels to Jerusalem to receive the city's surrender from Patriarch Sophronius and guarantees protection to its Christian inhabitants.

figures.name: Umar ibn al-Khattab
figures.role: Caliph; personally accepted surrender and signed Assurance of Safety

figures.name: Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah
figures.role: military commander who besieged the city

quranRef: Al-Isra 17:1 — reference to al-Masjid al-Aqsa
outcome: Jerusalem became Islam's third holiest city; Umar's covenant set precedent for religious tolerance

title: Conquest of Ctesiphon — fall of the Persian capital
description: Muslim forces capture Ctesiphon the magnificent Sassanid capital; the imperial treasury and the legendary Carpet of Khosrow fall into Muslim hands.
outcome: End of Sassanid power in Mesopotamia; massive treasure funded the early Islamic state

title: Founding of Kufa and Basra as garrison cities
description: Umar establishes Kufa and Basra as amsar (garrison cities) in Iraq creating the urban centres that will become major hubs of Islamic scholarship and culture.

figures.name: Umar ibn al-Khattab
figures.role: Caliph; ordered establishment of garrison cities

outcome: Created the cities that became centres of Islamic grammar, law, theology, and Shia thought

title: Deaths in 638 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 638 CE: S'ad bin 'Ubaid al-Qa'ri — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Qadisiya, Iraq, Qadisiya.

title: Umar establishes the Islamic calendar (Hijri)
description: Caliph Umar institutes the Hijri calendar dating from the Prophet's migration to Medina as the official calendar of the Muslim state.
outcome: Created Islam's distinctive calendar still used worldwide for religious observance

title: Conquest of the Jazira (upper Mesopotamia)
description: Muslim forces complete the conquest of upper Mesopotamia securing the fertile crescent and the strategic routes between Syria and Iraq.
outcome: Connected the Syrian and Iraqi fronts; Muslim empire became contiguous from Arabia to Anatolia

title: Deaths in 639 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 639 CE: Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah — Companion of the Prophet (Amin al-Umma — Trustee of the Nation) in Mecca / Syria; Fadl ibn al-'Abbas — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet) in Makkah / Amawas, Jerusalem; Muadh ibn Jabal — Companion of the Prophet (Imam al-Halal wa'l-Haram — Master of What is Permitted) in Medina / Yemen / Syria; Shurahbil bin Hasana — Companion of the Prophet (Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation) in Makkah / Amawas, Jerusalem; Utbah ibn Ghazwan — Companion of the Prophet (Founder of the City of Basra) in Makkah / Madinah / Basra (Iraq).

title: Amr ibn al-As begins conquest of Egypt
description: Amr ibn al-As leads 4000 troops into Egypt beginning the campaign that will end Byzantine rule over the Nile Valley.

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: commander; led the invasion of Egypt with 4000 men

outcome: Began the conquest that would bring the Nile's agricultural wealth into the Islamic world

title: Deaths in 640 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 640 CE: Bilal ibn Rabah — Companion of the Prophet (Sayyid al-Muadhdhinin — Master of the Callers to Prayer) in Mecca / Damascus, Syria; Khabbab Mawla 'Utbah bin Ghazwan — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medina; Qatada ibn al-Numan — Companion of the Prophet (Companion Whose Eye Was Restored by the Prophet) in Medina, Arabia; Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib — Female Companion (Aunt of the Prophet; Poet and Warrior at Uhud) in est., Makkah / Medinah; Usayd ibn Hudayr — Companion of the Prophet (Chief of Banu Abd al-Ashhal — Noble Ansari) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Conquest of Caesarea — last Byzantine holdout in Palestine
description: The fortified port of Caesarea the last major Byzantine stronghold in Palestine falls to Muslim forces after a prolonged siege.
outcome: Completed Muslim conquest of Palestine; all major ports now under Muslim control

title: Fall of Babylon Fortress and Alexandria
description: Amr ibn al-As captures the Babylon Fortress near modern Cairo and then takes Alexandria effectively ending Byzantine control of Egypt.

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: commander; conquered both Babylon Fortress and Alexandria

outcome: Egypt fell to Muslim control; Constantinople lost its richest province

title: Founding of Fustat (Old Cairo)
description: Amr ibn al-As establishes Fustat as the new administrative capital of Muslim Egypt and builds the first mosque in Africa (Mosque of Amr).

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: founder; established Fustat and built Africa's first mosque

outcome: Founded what would become Cairo — one of the greatest cities of the Islamic world

title: Deaths in 642 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 642 CE: Khalid ibn al-Walid — warrior (Sayf Allah al-Maslul — The Drawn Sword of God) in Mecca / Syria.

title: Battle of Nahavand — final defeat of Persia
description: The Muslims win a crushing victory at Nahavand in western Iran ending organized Sassanid resistance; it is called the Victory of Victories.

figures.name: Umar ibn al-Khattab
figures.role: Caliph; directed overall strategy from Medina

outcome: Ended organised Sassanid resistance; Iran lay open to Muslim armies

title: Deaths in 644 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 644 CE: Umar ibn al-Khattab — caliph (Al-Faruq — The Distinguisher; Second Caliph) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia.

title: Assassination of Caliph Umar
description: Umar ibn al-Khattab is stabbed by Abu Lu'lu'a a Persian slave during the dawn prayer in Medina; he dies three days later after appointing a six-man shura council.
outcome: End of the greatest administrative Caliph; his shura system set precedent for consultative succession

title: Uthman elected third Caliph by shura council
description: The six-man council appointed by the dying Umar selects Uthman ibn Affan as the third Caliph of Islam.
outcome: Uthman's election began a 12-year caliphate marked by both great expansion and growing internal tensions

title: Byzantine attempt to retake Alexandria fails
description: The Byzantines briefly recapture Alexandria but Amr ibn al-As returns and defeats them permanently securing Muslim control of Egypt.

figures.name: Amr ibn al-As
figures.role: returned to command; defeated Byzantine counter-attack

outcome: Last Byzantine attempt to recover Egypt failed; Muslim control became permanent

title: Deaths in 646 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 646 CE: al-Khansa — Female Companion (Greatest Female Poet of Arabic Literature; Mother of Four Martyrs) in Najd / Medinah; Hasana — Female Companion (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah; Jabir bin Sufyan bin Ma'mar — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah; Junadah bin Sufyan bin Ma'mar — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah; Ma'mar bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medina; Ma'mar bin Harith bin Ma'mar — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Sufyan bin Ma'mar — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah; Zaynab bint Jahsh — Female Companion (Zaynab bint Jahsh — Married by Divine Command) in Makkah / Medinah.

title: First Muslim naval raid on Cyprus
description: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan launches the first major Muslim naval expedition raiding Cyprus and signaling the emergence of Muslim sea power in the Mediterranean.

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: governor; launched the first Muslim naval campaign

outcome: Birth of Muslim naval power; Mediterranean was no longer a Byzantine lake

title: Deaths in 648 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 648 CE: Sawda bint Zama'a — Female Companion (First Wife after Khadijah; Mother of the Believers) in est., Makkah / Medinah; Umm Haram bint Milhan — Female Companion (Female Narrator (Rawyya) of Hadith) in Medinah / Cyprus.

title: Deaths in 649 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 649 CE: Ubayy ibn Ka'b — Companion of the Prophet (Sayyid al-Qurra — Master of Quran Reciters) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Conquest of Cyprus
description: Muawiya leads a full naval expedition and conquers Cyprus establishing Muslim naval dominance in the eastern Mediterranean.
outcome: Established permanent Muslim naval presence in the eastern Mediterranean

title: Deaths in 650 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 650 CE: Abdullah bin Ka'b — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Madinah; Abdullah bin Maz'un — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Abdullah bin Suraqa — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Abdullah ibn Hudhafah as-Sahmi — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Egypt; Abu Lubaba ibn Abd al-Mundhir — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Repentance — Tied Himself to the Mosque Pillar) in Medina, Arabia; Abu Sibrah bin Abi Ruhm — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah; Amr b Suraqa — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Habiba bint Sahl — Companion of the Prophet (Anṣārī Companion — First Khulʿ Case in Islam) in Medina; Jabbar bin Sakhr bin Umayya — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah; Khubayb bin Isaf — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Madinah; Malik bin Abi Khauliy — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Mu'adh bin 'Amr bin al-Jamuh — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Medina; Mu'ayqayb bin Abi Fatima al-Dausi — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Medinah; Qutba bin 'Amir bin Hadida — Companion of the Prophet (From the First Pledge of Aqabah) in Medinah; Yazid bin 'Amir bin Hadida — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah.

title: Uthman standardises the Quran (Uthmanic codex)
description: Caliph Uthman commissions Zayd ibn Thabit to produce a standard written Quran; copies are sent to major cities and variant manuscripts are destroyed.

figures.name: Zayd ibn Thabit
figures.role: chief scribe; led committee producing the standard text

quranRef: Al-Hijr 15:9 — 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the reminder and We will be its guardian'
outcome: Single authoritative Quran text preserved across the Muslim world; textual unity maintained for 1400 years

title: Fall of the Sassanid Empire
description: The last Sassanid emperor Yazdgerd III is killed in Merv ending over four centuries of Persian imperial rule and bringing Iran fully into the Islamic world.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; presided over the final conquest of Persia

outcome: End of the Sassanid Empire; Persian civilisation began its transformation within Islam

title: Deaths in 652 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 652 CE: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf — Companion of the Prophet (al-Sahabi al-Tajir — The Merchant Companion) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia; Abu al-Darda — Companion of the Prophet (Hakim al-Umma — Wise Man of the Nation) in Medina / Damascus, Syria; Abu Dharr al-Ghifari — Companion of the Prophet (Siddiq of This Umma — Prophet of the Poor) in Ghifar / Medina / Rabadha, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 653 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 653 CE: Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib — Companion of the Prophet (Sayyid Banu Hashim — Chief of Banu Hashim; Uncle of the Prophet) in Makkah / Madinah; Abdullah bin al-Husayn ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Nabi)) in Makkah / Madinah; Abdullah ibn Masud — Companion of the Prophet (First Hafiz of the Quran — Closest to the Prophet) in Mecca / Kufa, Iraq; al-Husayn ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Madinah; al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / al-Jurf, Medinah; al-Tufayl ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Madinah; Miqdad ibn Amr — Companion of the Prophet (First Cavalryman of Islam) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 654 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 654 CE: Abdullah bin Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Madinah; Abu Talha al-Ansari — Companion of the Prophet (Greatest Ansari Archer — Shield of the Prophet at Uhud) in Medina, Arabia; Arwa bint Kuraiz bin Rabi'a — Female Companion (Female Narrator (Rawyya) of Hadith) in Makkah / Medinah; Salman al-Farisi — Companion of the Prophet (Salman al-Khair — Salman the Good; Bridge of Two Worlds) in Isfahan / Medina / Ctesiphon; Ubada ibn al-Samit — Companion of the Prophet (First Judge of Palestine — Ansari Elder) in Medina / Jerusalem, Palestine.

title: Muslim forces reach the Indus River
description: Expeditionary forces under Uthman's caliphate push eastward into Makran and Sindh reaching the Indus Valley for the first time.
outcome: First Muslim contact with the Indian subcontinent; foreshadowed later Umayyad conquest of Sindh

title: Deaths in 655 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 655 CE: al-Harith ibn Nawfal ibn al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Companion and Governor) in Makkah / Basrah; Amir bin Rabi'a bin Malik — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Aws bin Khawli — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina; Umm al-Darda al-Sughra — Female Companion (Umm al-Darda — Greatest Female Scholar of Damascus) in Medinah / Syria; Umm al-Fadl Lubaba bint al-Harith — Female Companion (Second Woman to Accept Islam; Aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid) in Makkah / Medinah; Umm Mubashshir al-Ansariyyah — Companion of the Prophet (Anṣārī Companion — Witness to Prophetic Traditions) in Medina.

title: Battle of the Masts — first major naval battle
description: The Muslim fleet defeats a massive Byzantine armada off the Lycian coast in the Battle of the Masts establishing Muslim naval supremacy in the Mediterranean.

figures.name: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan
figures.role: organised the fleet; strategic architect of Muslim naval power

outcome: Byzantine naval supremacy broken; Muslims became the dominant Mediterranean naval power

title: Deaths in 656 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 656 CE: Abu 'Amra bin 'Amr bin Mihsan — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina / Siffin, Siffin; Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman — Companion of the Prophet (Sahib al-Sirr — Keeper of the Prophet's Secrets) in Medina / Kufa, Iraq; Qudamah bin Maz'un — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Medinah; Talha ibn Ubayd Allah — Companion of the Prophet (al-Fayyadh — The Generous; one of the Ten Promised Paradise) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia; Uthman ibn Affan — caliph (Dhul-Nurayn — Possessor of Two Lights; Third Caliph) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia; Zubayr ibn al-Awwam — Companion of the Prophet (Hawari al-Rasul — Disciple of the Prophet) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia.

title: Assassination of Caliph Uthman
description: Rebels from Egypt and Iraq besiege Uthman's house in Medina for weeks before storming it and killing him while he reads the Quran sparking the first civil war.

figures.name: Uthman ibn Affan
figures.role: Caliph; killed while reading the Quran

outcome: Sparked the First Fitna — the Muslim civil war that permanently divided the community

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib becomes fourth Caliph
description: Ali ibn Abi Talib is pledged allegiance as the fourth Caliph in Medina amid the chaos following Uthman's assassination.
outcome: Last of the Rashidun caliphs; his caliphate would be consumed by civil war

title: Battle of the Camel
description: Ali's forces clash with an army led by Aisha Talha and Zubayr near Basra over the question of punishing Uthman's killers; Ali prevails but the ummah is deeply divided.

figures.name: Aisha bint Abi Bakr
figures.role: led opposition; commanded from atop a camel

figures.name: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam
figures.role: opposition leader; withdrew then killed while retreating

outcome: Ali won but Muslim unity was shattered; first armed conflict between Companions

title: Deaths in 657 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 657 CE: Ammar ibn Yasir — Companion of the Prophet (First Martyr Family — Symbol of Early Persecution) in Mecca / Kufa, Iraq; Khabbab ibn al-Aratt — Companion of the Prophet (Among the First Seven — Early Martyr of Torture) in Kufa, Iraq; Uwais al-Qarani — Successor (Tabi'i) (Sayyid al-Tabieen — The Hidden Saint) in Qaran, Yemen / Kufa.

title: Battle of Siffin and the Arbitration
description: Ali and Muawiya's armies fight to a stalemate at Siffin on the Euphrates; the raising of Qurans on spears forces arbitration which weakens Ali's position.
outcome: Arbitration weakened Ali; Kharijites broke away; Muawiya's claim strengthened

title: Deaths in 658 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 658 CE: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Makkah / Kufa; Khuzayma bin Thabit — Companion of the Prophet (Martyr of the Battle of Uhud) in Medinah / Siffin, Jamal; Sahl bin Hunayf — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Kufa.

title: Emergence of the Kharijites
description: A faction of Ali's supporters reject the arbitration at Siffin and break away forming the Kharijites — the first sectarian movement in Islam.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; lost support from the Kharijite faction

outcome: First formal sectarian split in Islam; Kharijites would assassinate Ali and persist for centuries

title: Battle of Nahrawan
description: Ali defeats the Kharijite rebels at Nahrawan near Baghdad but their movement survives and radicalises leading to his eventual assassination.
outcome: Military victory but political failure; surviving Kharijites swore revenge

title: Deaths in 659 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 659 CE: Suhaib al-Rumi — Companion of the Prophet (Sahib al-Bayda' — The Byzantine Companion Who Bought His Freedom) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 660 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 660 CE: Abu Rafi — Companion of the Prophet (Freedman and Intimate Servant of the Prophet) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 661 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 661 CE: Ali ibn Abi Talib — caliph (Asad Allah al-Ghalib — Lion of God; Fourth Caliph) in Mecca / Kufa, Iraq; Tamim al-Dari — Companion of the Prophet (First to Introduce Mosque Lamps — Christian Convert) in Jerusalem / Medina; Umm Hani bint Abi Talib — Female Companion (Umm Hani bint Abi Talib; Witness to the Night Journey) in Makkah / Madinah.

title: Assassination of Ali ibn Abi Talib
description: Ali is struck by a poisoned Kharijite sword while praying at the mosque of Kufa; he dies two days later ending the era of the Rashidun Caliphs.

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Caliph; assassinated by Kharijite Ibn Muljam during prayer

outcome: End of the Rashidun caliphate; Imam Ali's shrine at Najaf became Shia Islam's holiest site

title: Muawiya establishes the Umayyad Caliphate
description: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan assumes the caliphate and moves the capital from Medina to Damascus founding the Umayyad dynasty that will rule for nearly a century.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: founded the Umayyad dynasty; moved capital to Damascus

outcome: Caliphate became hereditary monarchy; Damascus replaced Medina as the imperial capital

title: Hasan ibn Ali's treaty with Muawiya
description: Hasan ibn Ali cedes the caliphate to Muawiya in a peace treaty to avoid further Muslim bloodshed; the treaty includes conditions for succession after Muawiya's death.

figures.name: Hasan ibn Ali
figures.role: ceded caliphate to preserve Muslim unity

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: accepted treaty; promised to honour succession conditions

outcome: Preserved peace but Muawiya later broke succession terms by nominating Yazid

title: Deaths in 662 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 662 CE: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari — Companion of the Prophet (Ahsan al-Nas Sawtan — Most Beautiful Voice for Quran) in Yemen / Basra / Kufa, Iraq; Ka'b ibn Zuhayr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet and author of the Qasidat al-Burda) in Medina.

title: Ali ibn al-Husayn (Zayn al-Abidin) born
description: The son of Husayn ibn Ali and the great-grandson of the Prophet is born; he will become one of the most revered figures in Islamic spirituality and knowledge.

figures.name: Ali ibn al-Husayn
figures.role: born; future fourth Shia Imam and great scholar

outcome: Birth of the scholar-imam who would preserve the Prophet's family legacy after Karbala

title: Deaths in 663 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 663 CE: Abd Allah ibn Salam — Companion of the Prophet (Jewish Rabbi Who Recognized the Prophet) in Medina, Arabia; Usamah ibn Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam) in Makkah / Medinah.

title: Deaths in 664 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 664 CE: Amr ibn al-As — scholar (Conqueror of Egypt — Governor of Egypt) in Cairo (Fustat), Egypt.

title: Deaths in 665 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 665 CE: Hafsa bint Umar — Companion of the Prophet (Umm al-Muʾminīn — Custodian of the Quran) in Medina; Zayd ibn Thabit — Companion of the Prophet (Katib al-Wahy — Chief Scribe of Divine Revelation) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 666 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 666 CE: Muhammad ibn Maslama — Companion of the Prophet (Trusted Special Envoy and Swordsman of the Prophet) in Madinah, Arabia; Umm Habibah bint Abi Sufyan — Female Companion (Umm Habibah — Daughter of Abu Sufyan; Married by the Negus) in Makkah / Medinah.

title: Deaths in 667 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 667 CE: Umm Habib bint Jahsh — Female Companion (Female Narrator (Rawyya) of Hadith) in Makkah / Medinah.

title: Deaths in 669 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 669 CE: Hasan ibn Ali — scholar (al-Mujtaba — The Chosen One; Second Shia Imam) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Death of Hasan ibn Ali
description: Hasan ibn Ali the Prophet's grandson dies in Medina; many sources report he was poisoned marking a deepening of the Sunni-Shia schism over legitimate succession.
outcome: Removed the main obstacle to Muawiya's plans for hereditary succession

title: Deaths in 670 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 670 CE: Al-Mughira ibn Shu'ba — Companion of the Prophet (Shrewd Diplomat and Administrator of the Caliphate) in Kufa / Basra, Iraq; Fadalah wife of 'Abdullah bin 'Unays — Female Companion (Female Narrator (Rawyya) of Hadith) in Medinah; Ka'b ibn Malik — Companion of the Prophet (Poet-Companion Who Repented from Tabuk Absence) in Medina, Arabia; Saalam bin 'Umayr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Medina; Safiyya bint Huyayy — Female Companion (Safiyya bint Huyayy — From the Tribe of Aaron and Moses) in Medinah; Suraqa bin Ka'b bin 'Amr — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medinah / Medina.

title: Uqba ibn Nafi founds Kairouan
description: The Muslim general Uqba ibn Nafi establishes the city of Kairouan in modern Tunisia creating the base for the Islamisation of North Africa.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; directed the North African campaigns

outcome: Founded North Africa's most important Islamic city; base for the conquest of the Maghreb and Spain

title: Deaths in 671 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 671 CE: Ja'far ibn Abi Sufyan — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Prophet) in Makkah / Damascus, Syria; Sa'id ibn Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (One of the Ten Promised Paradise) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 673 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 673 CE: Fadala bin 'Ubaid bin Naqid — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Damascus, Syria; Imran bin Husain — Companion of the Prophet (Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam) in Hijaz / Basra; Imran ibn Husayn — Companion of the Prophet (Companion Who Heard Angels Greeting Him) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 674 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 674 CE: Abd Allah ibn Unays — Companion of the Prophet (Companion and Assassin of the Prophet's Enemy) in Medina / Egypt; Abdullah bin 'Unays al-Juhani — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah) in Medinah / Syria; Abu Ayyub al-Ansari — Companion of the Prophet (Host of the Prophet — Martyr before Constantinople) in Medina, Arabia / Constantinople; Abu Qatada al-Ansari — Companion of the Prophet (Faris Rasul Allah — Cavalry Commander of the Prophet) in Medina, Arabia; Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas — Companion of the Prophet (Faris al-Islam — Knight of Islam; Conqueror of Persia) in Mecca / Medina / Kufa, Iraq.

title: First Arab siege of Constantinople begins
description: Muawiya launches an ambitious naval siege of the Byzantine capital Constantinople; the siege lasts several years but ultimately fails against Greek fire defences.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; launched the siege seeking to conquer Constantinople

outcome: First major check on Arab expansion; Constantinople would not fall for another 800 years

title: Deaths in 676 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 676 CE: Zayd ibn Arqam — Companion of the Prophet (Narrator of Ghadir Khumm — Witness to Crucial Events) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 677 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 677 CE: Uqba bin 'Amir al-Juhayni — Companion of the Prophet (Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Nabi)) in HIjaz / Cario.

title: Deaths in 678 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 678 CE: Aisha bint Abi Bakr — Companion of the Prophet (Umm al-Mu'minin — Mother of the Believers) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia; Jarir ibn Abd Allah — Companion of the Prophet (Yusuf al-Umma — Most Handsome of the Companions) in Kufa, Iraq; Samura ibn Jundub — Companion of the Prophet (Companion-Judge of Basra) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Siege of Constantinople lifted
description: The Muslim fleet withdraws from Constantinople after Byzantine use of Greek fire inflicts devastating losses marking the first major check on Arab expansion.

figures.name: Mu'awiya I
figures.role: Caliph; forced to accept humiliating withdrawal

outcome: Forced Muawiya to sign a 30-year truce; saved Constantinople for Christendom

title: Deaths in 680 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 680 CE: Aqeel ibn Abi Talib — Companion of the Prophet (Brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib; Genealogist of Quraysh) in Makkah / Medinah; Husayn ibn Ali — scholar (al-Sayyid al-Shuhada — Master of Martyrs) in Medina / Karbala, Iraq; Mu'awiya I — ruler (Founder of the Umayyad Caliphate) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Death of Muawiya — Yazid I becomes Caliph
description: Muawiya dies having secured succession for his son Yazid breaking the precedent of consultative selection and establishing hereditary Umayyad rule.
outcome: Hereditary monarchy established; broke Hasan treaty terms; triggered Husayn's revolt

title: Battle of Karbala — martyrdom of Husayn
description: Husayn ibn Ali and 72 companions are massacred by Yazid's army at Karbala in Iraq; this event becomes the defining tragedy of Shia Islam and a symbol of resistance to tyranny.

figures.name: Husayn ibn Ali
figures.role: grandson of the Prophet; refused allegiance to Yazid; martyred

figures.name: Ali ibn al-Husayn
figures.role: Husayn's son; sole surviving male; taken prisoner

figures.name: Zaynab bint Ali
figures.role: Husayn's sister; gave defiant speeches in Damascus captivity

outcome: Permanent Sunni-Shia division crystallised; Ashura became annual day of mourning

title: Deaths in 681 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 681 CE: Abdullah bin Zayd Mazni — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Madinah, al-Harrah; Abu Hurairah — Companion of the Prophet (Hafiz al-Sahaba — Greatest Hadith Transmitter) in Yemen / Medina, Arabia; Abu Umama al-Bahili — Companion of the Prophet (Last Surviving Companion in Syria) in Syria; Jabir bin 'Atik — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of Badr) in Medina.

title: Deaths in 682 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 682 CE: Alqama ibn Qays — Successor (Tabi'i) (Head of the Kufan School of Jurisprudence) in Kufa, Iraq; Zaynab bint Ali — Female Companion (Aqila Banu Hashim — The Wise Woman of Banu Hashim; Voice of Karbala) in Medinah.

title: Deaths in 683 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 683 CE: Masruq ibn al-Ajda — Successor (Tabi'i) (Great Kufan Scholar and Companion of Aisha) in Kufa, Iraq; Sa'd bin Habban bin Munqidh — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Medina, al-Harrah; Wasay' bin Habban bin Munqidh — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Medina, al-Harrah; Yazid I — ruler (Second Umayyad Caliph — Battle of Karbala) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Revolt of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr in Mecca
description: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr declares himself Caliph in Mecca and controls the Hijaz Iraq and parts of Egypt challenging Umayyad authority for nearly a decade.

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Umayyad Caliph; sent army to suppress the revolt

outcome: Created a rival caliphate lasting 9 years; Mecca became centre of anti-Umayyad resistance

title: Umayyad army attacks Medina — Battle of al-Harra
description: Yazid's forces sack Medina after the city revolts; the three-day pillage horrifies the Muslim world and permanently damages Umayyad legitimacy.

figures.name: Yazid I
figures.role: Caliph; ordered the sack of the Prophet's city

outcome: Sack of the Prophet's city outraged Muslims; permanently damaged Umayyad religious legitimacy

title: First siege of Mecca — Kaaba damaged by fire
description: Umayyad forces besiege Mecca and catapult stones at the Kaaba causing a fire that damages the sacred structure; Yazid dies during the siege.

figures.name: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr
figures.role: defender; held Mecca against the siege

outcome: Kaaba damaged by catapult fire; Yazid's death ended the siege and plunged Umayyads into crisis

title: Deaths in 684 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 684 CE: Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn al-As — Companion of the Prophet (First Written Hadith Recorder) in Mecca / Egypt.

figures.name: Abd Allah ibn Amr ibn al-As

title: Death of Yazid I — Second Fitna intensifies
description: Yazid I dies after only three years of rule plunging the Umayyad caliphate into a succession crisis and multi-sided civil war.
outcome: Second Fitna escalated; Umayyad state nearly collapsed before Marwan I restored order

title: Deaths in 685 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 685 CE: al-Nu'man bin Bashir ibn Sa'd — Companion of the Prophet (Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam) in Medina / Hims; Ubaydallah bin al-'Abbas — Companion of the Prophet (Companion and Military Commander) in Makkah / Medina.

title: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan becomes Caliph
description: Abd al-Malik takes power in Damascus and spends a decade reasserting Umayyad authority over the fractured caliphate through military force and administrative genius.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: became Caliph; spent decade reunifying the caliphate

outcome: Saved the Umayyad dynasty; created the administrative infrastructure of the first Islamic empire

title: Deaths in 686 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 686 CE: Al-Aswad ibn Yazid — Successor (Tabi'i) (Kufan Jurist and Hadith Scholar) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 687 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 687 CE: Abdullah ibn Abbas — Companion of the Prophet (Hibr al-Umma — Scholar of the Umma; Father of Tafsir) in Mecca / Taif, Arabia; Adiyy ibn Hatim — Companion of the Prophet (Tribal Chief Who Came to the Prophet) in Kufa, Iraq; Jabir ibn 'Abdullah — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah) in Medinah / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 688 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 688 CE: Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali — scholar (Founder of Arabic grammar and inventor of diacritical marks) in Basra.

title: Deaths in 690 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 690 CE: Al-Bara ibn Azib — Companion of the Prophet (Companion and Battle Narrator) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 691 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 691 CE: Sahl ibn Sa'd — Companion of the Prophet (Last Companion in Medina — Witness to the Prophet's Era) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Dome of the Rock completed in Jerusalem
description: Caliph Abd al-Malik completes the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount in Jerusalem — the earliest surviving monumental work of Islamic architecture.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: patron and commissioner of the building

quranRef: Al-Isra 17:1 — the Night Journey to 'the farthest mosque'
sources: Oleg Grabar, The Dome of the Rock
outcome: Birth of Islamic monumental architecture; established the dome and Quranic mosaic as Islamic visual vocabulary

title: Hajjaj ibn Yusuf defeats Ibn al-Zubayr
description: The ruthless Umayyad general al-Hajjaj besieges Mecca and kills Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr ending the rival caliphate and reunifying the Umayyad state.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; sent Hajjaj to end the rival caliphate

figures.name: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr
figures.role: rival Caliph; killed after final stand at the Kaaba

outcome: Umayyad state reunified; Second Fitna ended; but Hajjaj's brutality left lasting bitterness

title: Deaths in 692 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 692 CE: Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr — Companion of the Prophet (Caliph of the Hijaz — Last Guardian of Mecca) in Medina / Mecca, Arabia; Abdullah bin Yazid bin Zayd — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Medinah / Kufa.

title: Arabic replaces Greek and Persian as administrative language
description: Abd al-Malik orders the Arabisation of the imperial bureaucracy replacing Greek in Syria and Persian in Iraq with Arabic as the sole language of government.
outcome: Arabic became the lingua franca of a civilisation stretching from Spain to Central Asia

title: Deaths in 693 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 693 CE: Abd Allah ibn Umar — Companion of the Prophet (Hafiz al-Sahaba — Great Hadith Preserver of Medina) in Mecca / Medina, Arabia; Abu Sa'id al-Khudri — Companion of the Prophet (Major Hadith Narrator of the Ansar) in Medina, Arabia; ibn Umar — Companion of the Prophet (Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18) in Makkah.

title: First purely Islamic coinage minted
description: Abd al-Malik introduces gold dinars and silver dirhams bearing Quranic inscriptions replacing Byzantine and Sassanid coins with distinctly Islamic currency.
outcome: Created the first Islamic monetary system; coins became vehicles for Quranic proclamation

title: Deaths in 694 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 694 CE: Jabir ibn Samura ibn Junadah al-Suwa'i — Companion of the Prophet.

figures.name: Jabir ibn Samura ibn Junadah al-Suwa'i

title: Al-Hajjaj appointed governor of Iraq
description: The iron-fisted Hajjaj ibn Yusuf is appointed governor of Iraq and the East; he crushes revolts and builds Wasit but is remembered for his brutality.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; appointed Hajjaj to pacify the East

outcome: Hajjaj crushed all opposition but his tyranny became a byword for oppressive rule in Islamic memory

title: Hasan al-Basri emerges as leading scholar of Basra
description: Hasan al-Basri becomes the most influential preacher and scholar of early Islam in Basra; his circle produces the foundations of Islamic theology and early asceticism.

figures.name: Hasan al-Basri
figures.role: scholar-preacher; founded the most important early Islamic intellectual circle

outcome: His students founded Islamic theology (Kalam), grammar, asceticism, and Mu'tazili thought

title: Deaths in 697 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 697 CE: Jabir ibn Abd Allah — Companion of the Prophet (Last of the Major Companions) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Conquest of Carthage — end of Byzantine North Africa
description: Muslim forces capture Carthage and expel the last Byzantines from North Africa opening the way for the full Islamisation of the Maghreb.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
figures.role: Caliph; directed the North African campaigns

outcome: End of Byzantine North Africa; cleared the path for the Muslim conquest of Spain 13 years later

title: Deaths in 699 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 699 CE: Shurayh al-Qadi — Successor (Tabi'i) (Greatest Judge of Early Islam — Sixty Years on the Bench) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 700 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 700 CE: Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyya — Successor (Tabi'i) (Son of Ali ibn Abi Talib — Respected Scholar) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Umayyad desert palaces flourish
description: The Umayyad caliphs build a network of luxurious desert palaces including Qusayr Amra and Mshatta across the Jordanian and Syrian steppe, featuring frescoes, mosaics, and baths that blend Greco-Roman and Sassanid artistic traditions.
sources: Oleg Grabar, Formation of Islamic Art
outcome: Created a unique Umayyad artistic synthesis of classical and Persian traditions

title: Deaths in 701 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 701 CE: Umar bin Abi Salamah — Companion of the Prophet (Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam) in Abyssinia or Medina / Medinah.

title: Deaths in 702 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 702 CE: Abd Allah ibn Ja'far — Companion of the Prophet (Sayyid al-Buhala' — Master of the Generous) in Ethiopia / Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 705 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 705 CE: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan — ruler (Builder of the Dome of the Rock — Arabiser of the Caliphate) in Damascus, Syria; Muhammad bin Hatib bin al-Harith — Companion of the Prophet (Emigrant to Abyssinia) in Makkah / Kufa.

title: Al-Walid I begins Umayyad architectural golden age
description: Caliph al-Walid I launches monumental building projects including expansion of the Prophet's Mosque, the Al-Aqsa Mosque, and the Great Mosque of Damascus.

figures.name: Al-Walid I
figures.role: Caliph; patron of the greatest Umayyad building programme

outcome: Created the masterpieces of Umayyad architecture

title: Great Mosque of Damascus constructed
description: Al-Walid I builds the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus on the site of the Cathedral of John the Baptist, with golden mosaics depicting paradise created by Byzantine craftsmen.
outcome: Prototype of congregational mosque architecture; mosaics among the finest Islamic art

title: Musa ibn Nusayr conquers western Maghreb
description: The Umayyad governor Musa ibn Nusayr completes the conquest of the western Maghreb, subduing Berber resistance and establishing Muslim control from Egypt to the Atlantic.
outcome: Secured the base for the invasion of Iberia the following year

title: Deaths in 711 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 711 CE: Jabir ibn Zayd — Successor (Tabi'i) (Theological founder of the Ibadi school) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses into Iberia
description: Berber commander Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses Gibraltar with 7,000 troops, defeats Visigothic King Roderic at Guadalete, and begins the Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula.
outcome: Began 800 years of Muslim civilisation in Iberia; Gibraltar = Jabal Tariq

title: Muhammad ibn Qasim conquers Sindh
description: The 17-year-old Umayyad general conquers Sindh, defeating Raja Dahir and establishing the first permanent Muslim rule in South Asia.
outcome: First permanent Muslim foothold in South Asia

title: Muslims conquer Samarkand and Bukhara
description: Qutayba ibn Muslim conquers the great Central Asian cities of Samarkand and Bukhara, bringing the Silk Road heartland under Umayyad control.
outcome: Brought Central Asia's intellectual and commercial centres into the Islamic world

title: Deaths in 713 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 713 CE: Ali ibn al-Husayn — Successor (Tabi'i) (Zayn al-Abidin — Ornament of the Worshippers; Author of al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya) in Karbala / Madinah; Anas ibn Malik — Companion of the Prophet (Khadim Rasul Allah — Servant of the Prophet) in Medina, Arabia; Urwa ibn al-Zubayr — Successor (Tabi'i) (First Islamic Historian — Transmitter of Sira) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 714 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 714 CE: Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf — ruler (Iron Governor of the East) in Wasit, Iraq; Ibrahim ibn Yazid al-Nakha'i — Successor (Tabi'i) in Kufa, Iraq; Sa'id ibn Jubayr al-Asadi al-Kufi — Successor (Tabi'i) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 715 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 715 CE: Al-Walid I — ruler (Builder of the Umayyad Mosque) in Damascus, Syria; Muhammad ibn al-Qasim — warrior (Conqueror of Sind — 17-Year-Old General) in Taif / Sind, Pakistan; Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib — Successor (Tabi'i) (Sayyid al-Tabi'in — Master of the Successor Generation) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Second Arab siege of Constantinople
description: Caliph Sulayman launches a massive siege of Constantinople with 120,000 troops and 1,800 ships; Greek fire and a harsh winter inflict catastrophic losses.
outcome: Second failure to take Constantinople; confirmed the city would remain Christian

title: Deaths in 717 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 717 CE: Amra bint Abd al-Rahman — scholar (Foremost Female Jurist of Medina) in Medina, Arabia; Amrah bint Abd al-Rahman — Successor (Tabi'i) (Baḥr al-ʿIlm — The Ocean of Knowledge) in Medina.

title: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz — the fifth Rashidun Caliph
description: Umar II implements sweeping reforms: equalising taxes between converts and Arabs, ending religious persecution, and governing with such justice that scholars call him the fifth Rashidun.

figures.name: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
figures.role: Caliph; implemented fiscal equality and justice reforms

outcome: Brief reign became the gold standard of righteous rule in Sunni political thought

title: Deaths in 718 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 718 CE: Sulayman ibn Yasar — Successor (Tabi'i) (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 720 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 720 CE: Kharija ibn Zayd ibn Thabit — Successor (Tabi'i) (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina) in Medina, Arabia; Tariq ibn Ziyad — warrior (Conqueror of Andalusia) in North Africa / Spain; Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz — ruler (Umar II — The Just Caliph, the Fifth Rightly-Guided) in Medina / Damascus; Hammam ibn Munabbih — Successor (Tabi'i) (Compiler of the Sahifah - earliest surviving hadith collection) in Sanaa, Yemen.

title: Death of Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
description: The reformist Caliph dies after only three years, possibly poisoned; his reforms are largely reversed.

figures.name: Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz
figures.role: died; possibly poisoned for his egalitarian reforms

outcome: End of the last attempt to reform the Umayyad state from within

title: Deaths in 722 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 722 CE: Mujahid ibn Jabr — Successor (Tabi'i) (Imam al-Tafsir — Master of Quranic Exegesis) in Mecca, Arabia.

title: Deaths in 723 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 723 CE: Ikrima Mawla Ibn Abbas — Successor (Tabi'i) (Chief Transmitter of Ibn Abbas's Tafsir) in Mecca / Khorasan.

title: Deaths in 724 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 724 CE: Tawus ibn Kaysan — Successor (Tabi'i) (Chief Jurist of Yemen) in Sanaa, Yemen.

title: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik becomes Caliph
description: Hisham assumes the caliphate and provides the last period of stable Umayyad rule, strengthening the army, reforming taxation, and expanding agriculture through irrigation projects.
outcome: Last effective Umayyad Caliph; his death precipitated the dynasty's collapse

title: Deaths in 728 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 728 CE: Hasan al-Basri — Successor (Tabi'i) (Sayyid al-Tabi'in) in Basra, Iraq; Qasim ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr — Successor (Tabi'i) (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina) in Medina, Arabia; Salim ibn Abd Allah ibn Umar — Successor (Tabi'i) (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina — Like His Grandfather Umar) in Medina, Arabia; Ubayd Allah ibn Abd Allah ibn Utba — Successor (Tabi'i) (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina — Poet-Scholar) in Medina, Arabia; Al-Farazdaq — poet (Major Umayyad poet famous for his rivalry with Jarir) in Basra; Jarir ibn Atiyah — poet (Major Umayyad poet and rival of al-Farazdaq) in Basra.

figures.name: Qasim ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr
Salim ibn Abd Allah ibn Umar
Ubayd Allah ibn Abd Allah ibn Utba

title: Death of Hasan al-Basri
description: The most influential early Islamic scholar dies; his students founded Islamic theology, grammar, asceticism, and the Mu'tazili school.
outcome: His legacy shaped Islamic theology, jurisprudence, and Sufism for all subsequent centuries

title: Wasil ibn Ata founds the Mu'tazila
description: Wasil ibn Ata separates from Hasan al-Basri's circle and establishes the Mu'tazili school of rational theology, which holds that the Quran is created and that reason can determine moral truth.
outcome: Founded the rationalist school that dominated Islamic theology for over a century

title: Deaths in 729 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 729 CE: Ibn Sirin — Successor (Tabi'i) (Imam al-Ta'bir — Master of Dream Interpretation) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 732 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 732 CE: Ata ibn Abi Rabah — Successor (Tabi'i) (Mufti of Mecca — Pilgrimage Authority) in Mecca, Arabia.

title: Battle of Tours/Poitiers
description: Charles Martel's Frankish army defeats the Umayyad force near Tours, marking the furthest Muslim military advance into western Europe.
outcome: Halted Muslim expansion into western Europe; Frankish power consolidated

title: Deaths in 733 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 733 CE: Muhammad al-Baqir — Successor (Tabi'i) (Al-Baqir — The Splitter of Knowledge; 5th Imam and Father of Islamic Sciences) in Madinah; al-Hakam ibn Utayba al-Kindi — Successor (Tabi'i) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 734 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 734 CE: Makhul al-Shami — Successor (Tabi'i) (Faqih al-Sham — Chief Jurist of Syria) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 735 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 735 CE: Qatada ibn Diama — Successor (Tabi'i) (Hafiz al-Basra — Prodigious Blind Memorizer) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 737 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 737 CE: Habib al-Ajami — Successor (Tabi'i) (First Persian Sufi — Student of Hasan al-Basri) in Basra, Iraq; Nafi Mawla Ibn Umar — Successor (Tabi'i) (The Golden Chain — Nafi' from Ibn Umar from the Prophet) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Khalid ibn Barmak enters Umayyad service
description: The Persian Buddhist-turned-Muslim Khalid ibn Barmak enters government service, beginning the rise of the Barmakid family that will dominate Abbasid administration for decades.
outcome: Began the Barmakid dynasty of viziers who shaped Abbasid golden age governance

title: Deaths in 740 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 740 CE: Zayd ibn Ali — scholar (Imam Zayd — Founder of Zaidi Jurisprudence) in Medina / Kufa, Iraq.

title: Great Berber Revolt
description: Berber Muslims revolt across North Africa against Arab taxation discrimination, defeating multiple Umayyad armies and ending direct Umayyad control of the Maghreb.
outcome: Broke Umayyad control of North Africa; led to independent Berber Muslim states

title: Zayd ibn Ali's revolt in Kufa
description: Zayd ibn Ali, grandson of Husayn, leads an armed revolt against the Umayyads in Kufa; he is killed in battle, giving rise to the Zaydi branch of Shia Islam.

figures.name: Zayd ibn Ali
figures.role: led revolt; killed; founded Zaydi Shia tradition

outcome: Founded the Zaydi branch of Shia Islam; Zaydi states later ruled Yemen for centuries

title: Deaths in 742 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 742 CE: Muhammad ibn Shihab al-Zuhri — Successor (Tabi'i) (Imam al-Hadith — Father of Systematic Hadith Collection) in Medina / Syria.

title: Deaths in 744 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 744 CE: Amr ibn Dinar al-Jumahi al-Makki — Successor (Tabi'i) in Mecca.

title: Umayyad succession crisis begins
description: The assassination of Caliph al-Walid II triggers a cascade of coups among Umayyad factions, fatally weakening the dynasty.
outcome: Umayyad dynasty consumed by internal strife; cleared the path for Abbasid revolution

title: Deaths in 745 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 745 CE: Yazid ibn Abi Habib Suwayd al-Azdi — Successor (Tabi'i) in Egypt.

figures.name: Yazid ibn Abi Habib Suwayd al-Azdi

title: Deaths in 747 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 747 CE: Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri — Successor (Tabi'i) in Medina.

figures.name: Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri

title: Abu Muslim launches the Abbasid revolution
description: Abu Muslim unfurls the black banners in Merv, rallying Arab settlers, Persian converts, and discontented Muslims against Umayyad rule.

figures.name: Abu Muslim
figures.role: revolutionary commander; launched the uprising in Khurasan

outcome: Began the revolution that destroyed the Umayyad caliphate

title: Deaths in 748 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 748 CE: Ayyub al-Sakhtiyani — Successor (Tabi'i) (Abū Bakr al-Baṣrī — Imām of the Hadith Scholars of Basra) in Basra; Yahya ibn Abi Kathir — Successor (Tabi'i) (Yemeni Hadith Scholar of Exceptional Memory) in Yamama, Arabia.

title: Battle of the Zab — Umayyads fall
description: Abbasid forces crush the last Umayyad army at the Great Zab River, ending 90 years of Umayyad rule.

figures.name: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah
figures.role: first Abbasid Caliph; destroyed Umayyad dynasty

outcome: Power shifted from Arab Damascus to cosmopolitan Baghdad

title: Massacre of the Umayyad family
description: Abbasids systematically kill nearly all Umayyad royals; one prince, Abd al-Rahman, escapes to Spain.

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: sole major survivor; fled to Spain

outcome: Near-extinction of Umayyads; the survivor founded Córdoba's emirate

title: Battle of Talas — paper reaches Islam
description: Abbasid forces defeat a Tang Chinese army at the Talas River; captured Chinese papermakers introduce paper technology to the Muslim world.
outcome: Paper replaced papyrus across the Islamic world, enabling the golden age of scholarship

title: Deaths in 754 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 754 CE: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah — caliph (1st Abbasid Caliph, founder of the Abbasid Caliphate) in Kufa / al-Anbar, Iraq.

title: Abu Muslim executed by al-Mansur
description: Caliph al-Mansur executes Abu Muslim, the revolutionary who made the Abbasid victory possible, fearing his immense power and popularity in Khurasan.

figures.name: Al-Mansur
figures.role: Caliph; ordered execution of the man who won him the throne

outcome: Eliminated the only figure who could challenge Abbasid authority; sparked revolts in Khurasan

title: Abd al-Rahman I founds Córdoba emirate
description: The lone Umayyad survivor reaches al-Andalus, defeats the Abbasid governor, and establishes an independent emirate that will rival Baghdad.

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: founded Umayyad Córdoba after fleeing the massacre

outcome: Independent Muslim civilisation in Iberia lasting 750 years

title: Deaths in 759 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 759 CE: Ibn al-Muqaffa — scholar (Father of Arabic Prose — Translator of Kalila wa Dimna) in Basra / Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 761 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 761 CE: Yahya ibn Sa'id ibn Qays al-Ansari — Successor (Tabi'i) in Medina; Sulayman ibn Tarkhan al-Taymi — Successor (Tabi'i) in Basra.

figures.name: Yahya ibn Sa'id ibn Qays al-Ansari

title: Deaths in 762 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 762 CE: Asim ibn Sulayman al-Ahwal — Successor (Tabi'i) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Baghdad founded as Abbasid capital
description: Caliph al-Mansur founds the Round City of Baghdad at the crossroads of trade routes; it rapidly becomes the world's largest and most cosmopolitan city.

figures.name: Al-Mansur
figures.role: Caliph; designed Baghdad as a circular planned city

outcome: Created the intellectual capital of the world for 500 years

title: Imam Malik begins teaching in Medina
description: Malik ibn Anas establishes his circle at the Prophet's Mosque in Medina, teaching hadith and jurisprudence that will form the basis of the Maliki school.

figures.name: Imam Malik ibn Anas
figures.role: began teaching the Muwatta in Medina

outcome: Founded the Maliki school — dominant in North and West Africa

title: Deaths in 763 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 763 CE: Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Qurashi al-Asadi — Successor (Tabi'i) in Medina.

figures.name: Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam al-Qurashi al-Asadi

title: Deaths in 765 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 765 CE: Ja'far al-Sadiq — scholar (al-Sadiq — The Truthful; Universal Teacher of the 8th Century) in Medina, Arabia; Amr ibn al-Harith ibn Ya'qub ibn Abdullah al-Ansari al-Misri — scholar in Egypt.

figures.name: Amr ibn al-Harith ibn Ya'qub ibn Abdullah al-Ansari al-Misri

title: Death of Ja'far al-Sadiq
description: The sixth Shia Imam dies; teacher of both Abu Hanifa and Malik ibn Anas, his students founded the Hanafi, Maliki, and Ja'fari schools.

figures.name: Ja'far al-Sadiq
figures.role: died; teacher of major Sunni and Shia jurists

outcome: Rare figure revered equally by Sunni and Shia; bridged both traditions

title: Deaths in 767 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 767 CE: Ibn Ishaq — scholar (First Biographer of the Prophet Muhammad) in Medina / Baghdad; Imam Abu Hanifa — jurist (al-Nu'man — Imam al-A'zam, The Greatest Imam) in Kufa / Baghdad.

title: Death of Abu Hanifa
description: Founder of the Hanafi school dies in Baghdad, reportedly imprisoned for refusing a government post.

figures.name: Abu Hanifa
figures.role: died; founder of the largest Islamic legal school

outcome: Hanafi school became dominant in Turkey, South Asia, and Central Asia

title: Ibn Ishaq completes the Sirah
description: Muhammad ibn Ishaq completes his biography of the Prophet, the earliest surviving Sirah, which becomes the foundational source for all later biographies.

figures.name: Ibn Ishaq
figures.role: author; completed the first comprehensive Prophet's biography

sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (via Ibn Hisham)
outcome: Created the foundational text of Prophetic biography used by all later historians

title: Deaths in 768 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 768 CE: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Jurayj al-Rumi al-Umawi al-Qurashi al-Makki — Successor (Tabi'i) in Mecca.

figures.name: Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Jurayj al-Rumi al-Umawi al-Qurashi al-Makki

title: Deaths in 770 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 770 CE: Ma'mar ibn Rashid al-Azdi al-San'ani — scholar in Basra.

title: Jabir ibn Hayyan pioneers experimental alchemy
description: Jabir ibn Hayyan (Geber), working under Ja'far al-Sadiq's intellectual legacy, develops systematic experimental methods in chemistry including distillation, crystallisation, and the classification of substances.

figures.name: Jabir ibn Hayyan
figures.role: pioneer; developed systematic experimental chemistry methods

outcome: Founded the experimental tradition in chemistry; Latin translations shaped European alchemy

title: Deaths in 771 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 771 CE: Hisham ibn Abi Abdullah Sinbar al-Ruba'i al-Basri al-Dastawa'i — scholar in Basra, Iraq.

figures.name: Hisham ibn Abi Abdullah Sinbar al-Ruba'i al-Basri al-Dastawa'i

title: Deaths in 773 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 773 CE: Sa'id ibn Abi Aruba — scholar in Basra.

title: Indian astronomical texts arrive in Baghdad
description: An Indian scholar brings the Siddhanta astronomical tables to the court of al-Mansur, sparking the Arabic translation of Indian mathematics including the decimal system and zero.
outcome: Introduced Indian numerals and zero to the Islamic world; later transmitted to Europe as 'Arabic numerals'

title: Deaths in 774 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 774 CE: Al-Awza'i — scholar (Imam Ahl al-Sham — The Syrian School of Law) in Beirut, Lebanon.

title: Deaths in 775 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 775 CE: Al-Mansur — ruler (Founder of Baghdad — Second Abbasid Caliph) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 777 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 777 CE: Ibrahim ibn Adham — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-Zuhhad — Sultan of Ascetics) in Balkh / Syria.

title: Deaths in 778 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 778 CE: Sufyan al-Thawri — scholar (Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith — Commander of the Faithful in Hadith) in Kufa / Basra / Mecca, Iraq.

title: Al-Khwarizmi born — father of algebra
description: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi is born in Khwarezm; he will write the book that gives algebra its name and introduce the Hindu-Arabic numeral system to the world.

figures.name: Al-Khwarizmi
figures.role: born; future father of algebra and algorithms

outcome: His name gave us 'algorithm'; his book gave us 'algebra'

title: Deaths in 781 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 781 CE: Dawud al-Ta'i — Sufi mystic (Imam al-Wara' — The Jurist Who Chose the Cave) in Kufa / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 784 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 784 CE: Hammad ibn Salama ibn Dinar al-Basri — scholar.

figures.name: Hammad ibn Salama ibn Dinar al-Basri

title: Deaths in 785 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 785 CE: Nafi' al-Madani — scholar (Imam al-Qurra — Master of Quranic Recitation) in Medina, Arabia.

title: Great Mosque of Córdoba begun
description: Abd al-Rahman I begins construction of the Great Mosque of Córdoba with its forest of double-arched columns.
outcome: One of the masterpieces of world architecture; symbol of Andalusian civilisation

title: Deaths in 786 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 786 CE: Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi — scholar (Inventor of Arabic prosody and compiler of first Arabic dictionary) in Basra.

title: Harun al-Rashid becomes Caliph
description: Harun al-Rashid begins the most celebrated reign in Islamic history: wealth, scholarship, diplomacy with Charlemagne, and the splendour of the Thousand and One Nights.

figures.name: Harun al-Rashid
figures.role: became Caliph; presided over Baghdad's golden age

outcome: Peak of Abbasid civilisation; Baghdad became the richest city on Earth

title: Barmakid family dominates Abbasid administration
description: The Persian Barmakid family, led by Yahya ibn Khalid and his sons Ja'far and al-Fadl, effectively run the Abbasid empire as viziers under Harun al-Rashid.
outcome: Model of Persian-Arab administrative synthesis; their fall became a legendary tale

title: Idris I founds the Idrisid dynasty in Morocco
description: Idris ibn Abdallah, a descendant of Ali and Hasan, escapes the Abbasid massacre of Alids and founds the first Shia-origin dynasty in Morocco, establishing Fez as his capital.
outcome: First independent Muslim dynasty in Morocco; founded Fez, future intellectual capital

title: Deaths in 791 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 791 CE: Al-Layth ibn Sa'd — scholar (Imam Egypt — Malik's Contemporary and Rival in Scholarship) in Egypt (Fustat); Idris ibn Abdallah — ruler (Founder of the Idrisid dynasty, first Islamic state in Morocco) in Fez.

title: Deaths in 793 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 793 CE: Abd al-Wahid ibn Zayd — Sufi mystic (Founder of the First Sufi Ribat) in Basra / 'Abbadan, Iraq.

title: Harun al-Rashid exchanges gifts with Charlemagne
description: Harun sends Charlemagne an elephant named Abul-Abbas, a water clock, and silk textiles, establishing the first diplomatic relationship between the Islamic caliphate and the Carolingian empire.
outcome: First direct diplomacy between the Islamic and Carolingian worlds

title: Deaths in 795 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 795 CE: Imam Malik ibn Anas — jurist (Imam Dar al-Hijra — Imam of Medina) in Medina, Arabia; Hammad ibn Zayd ibn Dirham — scholar in Basra.

title: Death of Malik ibn Anas
description: Malik ibn Anas, author of al-Muwatta and founder of the Maliki school, dies in Medina after decades as the city's foremost jurist.

figures.name: Imam Malik ibn Anas
figures.role: died; founder of the Maliki school

outcome: Maliki school became dominant in North Africa, West Africa, and parts of the Gulf

title: Deaths in 796 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 796 CE: Hisham I of Córdoba — ruler (Emir Hisham I — Established Maliki School in Al-Andalus) in Córdoba, Al-Andalus.

title: Harun al-Rashid destroys the Barmakids
description: Harun suddenly arrests and executes his vizier Ja'far al-Barmaki and imprisons the entire Barmakid family, ending decades of their dominance — one of the most dramatic falls in Islamic history.
outcome: Ended the Barmakid golden era; the fall became a proverb for the fickleness of power

title: Deaths in 797 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 797 CE: Abd Allah ibn al-Mubarak — scholar (Scholar-Warrior of Khorasan — Combined Jihad and Knowledge) in Merv / Khorasan.

title: Deaths in 798 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 798 CE: Abu Yusuf — jurist (Qāḍī al-Quḍāt — First Chief Judge of the Abbasid Caliphate) in Kufa / Baghdad.

title: Aghlabid dynasty established in Ifriqiya
description: The Abbasids appoint Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab as hereditary governor of Ifriqiya (Tunisia), founding the Aghlabid dynasty that will conquer Sicily and patronise scholarship.
outcome: Founded the dynasty that conquered Sicily and built Kairouan's golden age

title: Deaths in 800 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 800 CE: Ibrahim ibn Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri — scholar in Medina.

figures.name: Ibrahim ibn Sa'd ibn Ibrahim ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf al-Zuhri

title: Harun al-Rashid at the height of his power
description: Baghdad under Harun al-Rashid is the wealthiest and most cultured city on Earth with a population approaching one million, surpassing Constantinople and dwarfing any European city.
outcome: Baghdad's population and wealth exceeded anything in the contemporary world

title: Deaths in 801 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 801 CE: Rabia al-Basri — Sufi mystic (Umm al-Khayr — Mother of Good) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 803 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 803 CE: Fudayl ibn Iyad — Sufi mystic (Imam al-Haramayn — Imam of the Two Sanctuaries) in Khorasan / Mecca.

title: Fall of the Barmakids complete
description: The once-mighty Barmakid family is destroyed: Ja'far executed, Yahya imprisoned, their vast fortune confiscated. The fall becomes a proverbial tale of power's fickleness.
outcome: Ended the most effective administrative dynasty of the Abbasid era

title: Deaths in 805 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 805 CE: Muhammad al-Shaybani — jurist (Shaybani — Systematiser of Hanafi Law and Father of Islamic International Law) in Wasit / Baghdad.

title: Al-Shafi'i develops systematic legal theory
description: Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i writes al-Risala, the first systematic work on Islamic legal methodology (usul al-fiqh), establishing the four sources of law: Quran, Sunnah, ijma, and qiyas.

figures.name: Imam al-Shafi'i
figures.role: author; created systematic Islamic legal theory

outcome: Created the science of Islamic legal theory that all subsequent schools follow

title: Deaths in 806 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 806 CE: Ibn al-Qasim — jurist (Primary Transmitter of the Maliki School in Egypt) in Egypt / Medina.

title: Deaths in 809 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 809 CE: Harun al-Rashid — ruler (Amir al-Muminin — Caliph of the Golden Age) in Baghdad, Iraq; Muhammad ibn Ja'far al-Hudhali al-Basri al-Karabisi — scholar in Basra, Iraq.

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Ja'far al-Hudhali al-Basri al-Karabisi

title: Death of Harun al-Rashid
description: Harun al-Rashid dies on campaign in Khurasan; his death triggers a civil war between his sons al-Amin and al-Ma'mun that devastates Baghdad.
outcome: His death split the empire between his Arab-backed and Persian-backed sons

title: Deaths in 810 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 810 CE: Hafs ibn Ghiyath ibn Talq al-Nakha'i — scholar in Kufa, Iraq.

figures.name: Hafs ibn Ghiyath ibn Talq al-Nakha'i

title: Deaths in 812 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 812 CE: Waki ibn al-Jarrah — scholar (Teacher of al-Shafi'i and Ahmad — Master of Memory) in Kufa, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 813 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 813 CE: Abd al-Rahman ibn Mahdi — scholar (Imam al-Hadith — Critic Who Shaped All Subsequent Hadith Science) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Al-Ma'mun wins civil war — siege of Baghdad
description: Al-Ma'mun's general Tahir ibn Husayn besieges Baghdad for over a year; al-Amin is killed, and al-Ma'mun becomes Caliph from his base in Merv before eventually moving to Baghdad.
outcome: Baghdad devastated by siege; al-Ma'mun became the most intellectually ambitious Caliph

title: Deaths in 814 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 814 CE: Sufyan ibn Uyayna — scholar (Greatest Meccan Hadith Scholar of His Age) in Mecca, Arabia; Abu Nuwas — poet (Greatest bacchic poet of classical Arabic literature) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 815 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 815 CE: Abu Sulayman al-Darani — Sufi mystic (Imam al-Sham — Imam of Syria) in Damascus, Syria; Jabir ibn Hayyan — scientist (Father of Chemistry — Geber) in Tus / Kufa, Iraq; Ma'ruf al-Karkhi — Sufi mystic (Qutb Baghdad — Teacher of Sari al-Saqati) in Karkh / Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Al-Ma'mun establishes the Bayt al-Hikma
description: Caliph al-Ma'mun formally establishes the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad as a major centre for translating Greek, Persian, Sanskrit, and Syriac texts into Arabic.
sources: Dimitri Gutas, Greek Thought, Arabic Culture
outcome: Launched the greatest translation movement in history; preserved Greek philosophy for humanity

title: Deaths in 817 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 817 CE: Hammad ibn Usama ibn Zayd ibn Sulayman al-Qurashi al-Kufi — scholar in Kufa, Iraq.

figures.name: Hammad ibn Usama ibn Zayd ibn Sulayman al-Qurashi al-Kufi

title: Deaths in 818 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 818 CE: Ali al-Ridha — scholar (Imam Ali al-Ridha — Eighth Imam of Twelver Shia) in Medina / Mashhad, Iran; Yahya ibn Sa'id al-Qattan — scholar (Father of Hadith Criticism — Standard of Basra) in Basra, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 819 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 819 CE: Hisham ibn al-Kalbi — scholar (Arab genealogist and historian of pre-Islamic Arabia) in Kufa.

title: Deaths in 820 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 820 CE: Imam al-Shafi'i — jurist (Nasir al-Sunna — Founder of Legal Theory) in Gaza / Mecca / Cairo.

title: Al-Khwarizmi writes Kitab al-Jabr
description: Al-Khwarizmi publishes The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing, the founding text of algebra, dedicated to Caliph al-Ma'mun.

figures.name: Al-Khwarizmi
figures.role: author; wrote the book that named algebra

outcome: Gave algebra its name; his work on Hindu numerals gave us 'algorithm'

title: Deaths in 821 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 821 CE: Yazid ibn Harun ibn Zadhi al-Sulami al-Wasiti — scholar in Wasit, Iraq.

figures.name: Yazid ibn Harun ibn Zadhi al-Sulami al-Wasiti

title: Deaths in 824 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 824 CE: Nafisa bint al-Hasan — Sufi mystic (Nafisa al-'Ilm — Jewel of Knowledge) in Medina / Cairo, Egypt.

title: Hunayn ibn Ishaq leads the translation movement
description: The Nestorian Christian scholar Hunayn ibn Ishaq becomes the chief translator at the House of Wisdom, systematically translating Galen, Hippocrates, Plato, and Aristotle into Arabic.
outcome: Translated the entire Greek medical and philosophical corpus into Arabic

title: Deaths in 826 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 826 CE: Abu-l-Atahiya — poet (Pioneer of ascetic poetry (zuhdiyyat) in Arabic literature) in Kufa.

title: Al-Ma'mun imposes Mu'tazili doctrine as state creed
description: Caliph al-Ma'mun declares the Mu'tazili rationalist position — that the Quran is created, not eternal — as official state doctrine, launching the Mihna (inquisition) against dissenters.
outcome: Began the Mihna inquisition; Ahmad ibn Hanbal's resistance became legendary

title: Deaths in 828 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 828 CE: Asad ibn al-Furat — scholar (Maliki jurist who led the Muslim conquest of Sicily) in Kairouan.

title: Ahmad ibn Hanbal defies the Mihna
description: Ahmad ibn Hanbal refuses to accept the Mu'tazili creed under interrogation and imprisonment, enduring flogging and years of detention rather than compromise his belief in the uncreated Quran.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Hanbal
figures.role: scholar; defied the Mihna inquisition, endured imprisonment and flogging

outcome: His resistance made him a hero of Sunni orthodoxy; Hanbali school founded on his legacy

title: Palermo falls to Aghlabid Muslims
description: The Aghlabid dynasty completes the conquest of Palermo, making it the capital of Muslim Sicily; the island becomes a centre of Arab-Norman cultural synthesis.
sources: Amari, Storia dei Musulmani di Sicilia
outcome: Muslim Sicily became a model of multicultural coexistence under Arab, then Norman rule

title: Deaths in 833 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 833 CE: Ibn Hisham — scholar (Editor of the Canonical Sira of the Prophet) in Basra / Cairo; Ma'mun al-Rashid — ruler (Founder of the House of Wisdom) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Death of al-Ma'mun
description: The most intellectually engaged Caliph dies on campaign against Byzantium; his legacy is the House of Wisdom and the translation movement that preserved Greek learning.
outcome: Left behind the greatest intellectual institution of the medieval world

title: Deaths in 835 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 835 CE: Muhammad al-Jawad — scholar (Imam al-Jawad — Ninth Imam; Youngest Imam at Age 9) in Medina / Baghdad.
