title: Samarra founded as new Abbasid capital
description: Caliph al-Mu'tasim moves the capital from Baghdad to the new city of Samarra on the Tigris to house his Turkish slave-soldier army away from Baghdad's hostile population.
outcome: Created a new capital with monumental architecture including the famous spiral minaret

title: Deaths in 838 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 838 CE: Fatima of Nishapur — Sufi mystic (Umm al-Fuqara — Mother of the Poor) in Nishapur, Khorasan; Sulayman ibn Harb al-Azdi — scholar in Basra.

title: Sack of Amorion — last great Abbasid offensive
description: Caliph al-Mu'tasim leads 200,000 troops to sack the Byzantine city of Amorion in Anatolia, avenging the Byzantine sack of Zapetra; it is the last great Abbasid military victory against Byzantium.
outcome: Last major Abbasid offensive victory; the empire would turn inward thereafter

title: Deaths in 840 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 840 CE: Sulayman ibn Dawud ibn Dawud — scholar.

title: Al-Kindi writes On First Philosophy
description: Ya'qub al-Kindi, the 'Philosopher of the Arabs,' writes On First Philosophy, the first systematic work of Islamic philosophy, reconciling Greek rationalism with Islamic revelation.

figures.name: Al-Kindi
figures.role: author; first Arab philosopher to systematise Greek thought in Islamic terms

outcome: Founded the Islamic philosophical tradition that led to Ibn Sina and Ibn Rushd

title: Deaths in 841 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 841 CE: Bishr al-Hafi — Sufi mystic (al-Hafi — The Barefoot Saint of Baghdad) in Merv / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 845 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 845 CE: Ibrahim al-Nazzam — philosopher (Philosopher of Basra) in Basra.

title: Deaths in 846 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 846 CE: Abu Tammam — poet (Abbasid poet and compiler of the Hamasa anthology) in Damascus.

title: Mihna ends — traditionalist Islam triumphs
description: The Abbasid Caliph al-Mutawakkil formally ends the Mihna inquisition, abandoning the Mu'tazili state creed and restoring the traditionalist position that the Quran is uncreated.
outcome: Sunni orthodoxy established permanently; Ahmad ibn Hanbal vindicated

title: Al-Bukhari completes his Sahih
description: Muhammad al-Bukhari completes Sahih al-Bukhari, selecting 7,275 hadith from over 600,000 examined, creating the work universally regarded as the most authentic collection of prophetic traditions.

figures.name: Al-Bukhari
figures.role: author; compiled the most authoritative hadith collection

outcome: Created the gold standard of hadith authentication; most trusted book after the Quran in Sunni Islam

title: Deaths in 848 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 848 CE: Abu Muhammad Yahya ibn Yahya ibn Kathir ibn Wislasen al-Laythi — scholar in Algeciras, al-Andalus.

figures.name: Abu Muhammad Yahya ibn Yahya ibn Kathir ibn Wislasen al-Laythi

title: Deaths in 849 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 849 CE: Zuhayr ibn Harb ibn Shaddad al-Nasa'i — scholar in Nasa, Khurasan; Ali ibn Abdullah ibn Ja'far al-Madini — scholar in Basra, Iraq.

figures.name: Zuhayr ibn Harb ibn Shaddad al-Nasa'i
Ali ibn Abdullah ibn Ja'far al-Madini

title: Deaths in 850 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 850 CE: Al-Khwarizmi — scientist (Father of Algebra — Inventor of the Algorithm) in Khwarezm / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 853 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 853 CE: Ishaq ibn Ibrahim ibn Makhlad ibn Rahwayh al-Hanzali al-Marwazi — scholar in Merv (present-day Mary, Turkmenistan).

figures.name: Ishaq ibn Ibrahim ibn Makhlad ibn Rahwayh al-Hanzali al-Marwazi

title: Deaths in 854 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 854 CE: Sahnun — jurist (Compiler of Al-Mudawwana — The Constitution of Maliki Law) in Kairouan, Tunisia; Qutayba ibn Sa'id al-Balkhi — scholar in Baghlan (Balkh region).

title: Deaths in 855 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 855 CE: Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal — jurist (Imam Ahl al-Sunna — The Imam Who Refused to Capitulate) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Hanbal
description: Ahmad ibn Hanbal, hero of the Mihna and founder of the Hanbali school, dies in Baghdad; his funeral is attended by hundreds of thousands.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Hanbal
figures.role: died; his funeral drew one of the largest gatherings in Baghdad's history

outcome: Founded the Hanbali school; his legacy shaped Sunni orthodoxy, later influencing Wahhabism

title: Deaths in 857 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 857 CE: Harith al-Muhasibi — scholar (al-Muhasibi — The Self-Examiner) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 859 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 859 CE: Dhul-Nun al-Misri — Sufi mystic (Imam al-'Arifin — Imam of the Gnostics) in Akhmim / Cairo, Egypt.

title: University of al-Qarawiyyin founded in Fez
description: Fatima al-Fihri, a woman from Kairouan, founds the University of al-Qarawiyyin in Fez, Morocco — recognised by UNESCO and Guinness as the world's oldest continuously operating university.

figures.name: Fatima al-Fihri
figures.role: founder; established the world's oldest university

outcome: World's oldest continuously operating university; centre of Islamic scholarship for over 1,000 years

title: Deaths in 861 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 861 CE: Al-Mutawakkil — ruler (al-Mutawakkil — Abbasid Caliph who Ended the Mihna) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Assassination of Caliph al-Mutawakkil by Turkish guards
description: Turkish military commanders assassinate Caliph al-Mutawakkil in Samarra, beginning an era where Turkish slave-soldiers control the caliphate and make or break Caliphs at will.
outcome: Began the 'Anarchy at Samarra'; Caliphs became puppets of their own military

title: Deaths in 862 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 862 CE: Ahmad ibn Salih al-Misri (Ibn al-Tabari) — scholar in Egypt.

title: Deaths in 867 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 867 CE: Sari al-Saqati — Sufi mystic (Shaykh Baghdad — Teacher of Junayd) in Baghdad, Iraq; Ahmad ibn Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-Sarh al-Qurashi al-Umawi — scholar in Egypt.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-Sarh al-Qurashi al-Umawi

title: Deaths in 868 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 868 CE: Al-Jahiz — scholar (al-Jāḥiẓ — Father of Arabic Literature) in Basra / Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Ahmad ibn Tulun founds the Tulunid dynasty in Egypt
description: The Turkish commander Ahmad ibn Tulun declares effective independence from the Abbasids and founds the Tulunid dynasty in Egypt, building a magnificent new capital and mosque.
outcome: First independent Muslim dynasty in Egypt since the conquest; model for later Fatimid state

title: Deaths in 869 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 869 CE: Ahmad ibn Mani' ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Baghawi — scholar in Marw al-Rudh (Merv), Khorasan.

figures.name: Ahmad ibn Mani' ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Baghawi

title: Deaths in 870 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 870 CE: Al-Farghani — scientist (al-Farghānī — Pioneer of Astronomy) in Farghana / Baghdad / Cairo; Imam al-Bukhari — scholar (Amir al-Mu'minin fi'l-Hadith — Prince of Hadith) in Bukhara / Samarkand; Habash al-Hasib al-Marwazi — scientist (Astronomer who compiled influential zij tables and introduced trigonometric functions) in Baghdad.

title: Al-Tirmidhi and Abu Dawud complete hadith collections
description: Two of the six canonical Sunni hadith collections are completed: Sunan al-Tirmidhi and Sunan Abu Dawud, rounding out the 'Six Books' alongside Bukhari and Muslim.
outcome: Completed the canonical hadith corpus that forms the basis of Sunni legal and theological reasoning

title: Deaths in 873 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 873 CE: Al-Kindi — philosopher (Faylasuf al-Arab — The Philosopher of the Arabs) in Kufa / Baghdad; Banu Musa — scientist (Three Brothers — Pioneers of Mechanics and Mathematics) in Baghdad, Iraq; Hunayn ibn Ishaq — scientist (Chief Translator of the House of Wisdom) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 874 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 874 CE: Abu Yazid al-Bistami — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-'Arifin — Sultan of the Gnostics) in Bistam, Iran.

title: Twelfth Imam goes into occultation — Shia theology transformed
description: According to Twelver Shia belief, the twelfth Imam Muhammad al-Mahdi goes into occultation (ghayba) at age five, beginning the Lesser Occultation and fundamentally shaping Shia eschatology.
outcome: Defined Twelver Shia theology and eschatology; the awaited Mahdi remains central to Shia belief

title: Deaths in 875 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 875 CE: Imam Muslim — scholar (Imam al-Sahih — Student of al-Bukhari) in Nishapur, Iran.

title: Zanj Revolt threatens Abbasid heartland
description: A massive revolt of East African slaves (Zanj) in southern Iraq under Ali ibn Muhammad establishes an independent state for 14 years, sacking Basra and threatening Baghdad.
outcome: Largest slave revolt in Islamic history; devastated southern Iraq's agricultural economy

title: Deaths in 877 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 877 CE: Al-Muzani — jurist (Imam al-Muzani — Greatest Student of al-Shafi'i) in Egypt / Cairo.

title: Ibn Tulun Mosque completed in Cairo
description: Ahmad ibn Tulun completes his grand mosque in al-Qata'i (Cairo), with its distinctive spiral minaret inspired by the Malwiya of Samarra — one of the oldest intact mosques in Egypt.
outcome: One of Egypt's greatest architectural monuments; oldest intact mosque in Cairo

title: Deaths in 880 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 880 CE: Fatima al-Fihri — founder (Umm al-Banin — Founder of Al-Qarawiyyin) in Fez, Morocco.

title: Al-Farabi born — Second Teacher after Aristotle
description: Abu Nasr al-Farabi is born in Turkestan; he will be called 'the Second Teacher' (after Aristotle) for his synthesis of Greek philosophy with Islamic thought.

figures.name: Al-Farabi
figures.role: born; future 'Second Teacher' of Islamic philosophy

outcome: His political philosophy and logic shaped all subsequent Islamic and European thought

title: Samanid dynasty rises as champions of Persian culture
description: The Samanid dynasty consolidates power over Transoxiana and Khurasan from their capital Bukhara, becoming the great patrons of Persian literature and the New Persian language.
outcome: Launched the Persian literary renaissance; their court produced Firdawsi's Shahnameh tradition

title: Deaths in 886 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 886 CE: Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi — scientist (Albumasar — Greatest Islamic Astrologer) in Balkh / Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 887 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 887 CE: Ibn Majah — scholar (Imam al-Hafiz — Sixth of the Six Masters) in Qazvin, Iran; Abbas ibn Firnas — scientist (Andalusian polymath and aviation pioneer) in Cordoba.

title: Deaths in 889 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 889 CE: Abu Dawud — scholar (Imam al-Sunan — Master of the Prophetic Practices) in Sijistan / Basra / Baghdad.

title: Al-Tabari begins writing his universal history
description: Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari begins composing his monumental Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk (History of Prophets and Kings), the most comprehensive work of Islamic historiography ever written.

figures.name: Al-Tabari
figures.role: author; wrote the definitive Islamic universal history

outcome: Created the foundational text of Islamic historiography used by all subsequent historians

title: Deaths in 892 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 892 CE: Al-Baladhuri — scholar (Author of Futuh al-Buldan — Islamic Conquest Historian) in Baghdad, Iraq; Muhammad al-Tirmidhi — scholar (Imam al-'Ilal — Master of Hadith Analysis) in Tirmidh (Termez), Uzbekistan.

title: Abbasid capital returns to Baghdad
description: After nearly 60 years in Samarra, the Abbasid court permanently returns to Baghdad under Caliph al-Mu'tadid, though by now the Caliphs control little beyond central Iraq.
outcome: Baghdad regained its status as capital, but the Abbasid empire had fragmented irreversibly

title: Deaths in 895 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 895 CE: Dinawari — scientist (Persian polymath and father of Arabic botany) in Dinavar.

title: Deaths in 896 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 896 CE: Sahl al-Tustari — Successor (Tabi'i) (Qutb al-Tafsir — Pole of Quranic Exegesis) in Shushtar / Basra; Ibn al-Rumi — poet (Major Abbasid poet known for descriptive and philosophical verse) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 897 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 897 CE: Al-Yaqubi — scholar (First Universal Islamic Historian) in Baghdad / Egypt / Khorasan.

title: Ismaili Shia missionary network spreads across Muslim world
description: The Ismaili da'wa (missionary network) operates secretly from Yemen to North Africa, preparing the ground for the Fatimid revolution that will create a rival caliphate.
outcome: Laid the groundwork for the Fatimid caliphate — Islam's most successful revolutionary movement

title: Deaths in 901 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 901 CE: Thabit ibn Qurra — scientist (Renovator of Hellenistic Knowledge) in Harran / Baghdad.

title: Samanids patronise New Persian literature
description: Under Samanid patronage, the New Persian literary tradition flourishes; Rudaki, the 'father of Persian poetry,' composes at the Bukhara court, and Bal'ami translates Tabari's history into Persian.
outcome: Birth of the Persian literary tradition that produced Firdawsi, Rumi, Hafez

title: Deaths in 907 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 907 CE: Abu al-Husayn al-Nuri — Sufi mystic (al-Nuri — The Luminous; Martyr of Love) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Fatimid Caliphate established in North Africa
description: The Ismaili Shia imam Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi overthrows the Aghlabids and establishes the Fatimid Caliphate in Ifriqiya, creating a rival to the Abbasid caliphate.
outcome: Created the only Shia caliphate in history; rival to both Abbasids and Umayyads of Córdoba

title: Deaths in 910 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 910 CE: Hakim al-Tirmidhi — scholar (al-Hakim — Imam of the Science of Sainthood) in Tirmidh, Central Asia; Junayd of Baghdad — Sufi mystic (Sayyid al-Ta'ifa — Master of the Sufi Group) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 912 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 912 CE: Ibn Khordadbeh — scholar (First Islamic Geographer — Book of Routes and Realms) in Khurasan / Baghdad; Qusta ibn Luqa — scientist (Christian Polymath of the Islamic Golden Age) in Baalbek / Baghdad; Ahmad ibn Yusuf — scientist (Arab mathematician who wrote on ratios and proportions) in Baghdad.

title: Abd al-Rahman III becomes Emir of Córdoba
description: The greatest Umayyad ruler of al-Andalus begins his 49-year reign, transforming Córdoba into the most advanced city in Europe.
outcome: Began the golden age of al-Andalus; Córdoba rivalled Baghdad and Constantinople

title: Deaths in 915 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 915 CE: Al-Nasa'i — scholar (Imam al-Naqd — Most Precise of the Six Masters) in Nasa (Khorasan) / Egypt / Damascus; Al-Jubba'i — philosopher (Philosopher of Basra) in Basra.

title: Deaths in 918 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 918 CE: Ibn Surayj — scholar (al-Shafi'i al-Saghir — The Second Shafi'i) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 922 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 922 CE: Al-Hallaj — Sufi mystic (Shahid al-'Ishq — Martyr of Love) in Fars / Baghdad; Ibn 'Ata al-Adami — Sufi mystic (Shahid al-Mahabba — Martyr of Love) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Execution of al-Hallaj in Baghdad
description: The mystic Husayn ibn Mansur al-Hallaj is executed in Baghdad for declaring 'Ana al-Haqq' (I am the Truth/God), becoming the most famous martyr of Sufism.

figures.name: Al-Hallaj
figures.role: Sufi mystic; executed for his ecstatic declaration 'I am the Truth'

outcome: Became Sufism's most famous martyr; his death defined the tension between mysticism and orthodoxy

title: Deaths in 923 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 923 CE: Al-Tabari — historian (Imam al-Mufassirin — The Imam of Quranic Commentary) in Tabaristan / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 925 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 925 CE: Al-Razi (Rhazes) — scientist (Galen of the Arabs — Father of Clinical Medicine) in Ray (near Tehran), Iran.

title: Deaths in 929 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 929 CE: Al-Battani — scientist (Albategnius — Greatest Arab Astronomer) in Raqqa / Antioch, Syria.

title: Abd al-Rahman III declares himself Caliph
description: The Umayyad emir of Córdoba takes the title of Caliph, creating a third caliphate alongside the Abbasids and Fatimids — the Islamic world now has three rival Caliphs.
outcome: Three simultaneous caliphates; Córdoba declared equal to Baghdad and the Fatimid capital

title: Deaths in 930 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 930 CE: Abu Kamil — scientist (Egyptian mathematician who advanced al-Khwarizmi's algebra) in Cairo; Ibn Rustah — scholar (Persian geographer who compiled al-Alaq al-Nafisa on peoples and lands) in Isfahan.

title: Deaths in 931 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 931 CE: Ibn Masarra — philosopher (First Philosopher of Andalusia) in Córdoba, Andalusia.

title: Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad
description: The Shia Iranian Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad and reduces the Abbasid Caliph to a puppet; for the first time, a Shia dynasty controls the Sunni caliphate's capital.
outcome: Abbasid Caliphs became powerless figureheads; real power held by Buyid military commanders

title: Deaths in 933 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 933 CE: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi — philosopher (Philosopher of Tehran) in Tehran; Ibn Duraid — scholar (Arab lexicographer and author of Kitab al-Jamhara) in Basra.

figures.name: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi

title: Deaths in 934 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 934 CE: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi — ruler (Founder of the Fatimid Caliphate) in Syria / Mahdia, Tunisia; Abu Zayd al-Balkhi — scientist (Persian polymath and pioneer of mental health geography) in Balkh.

title: Deaths in 935 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 935 CE: Al-Tahawi — scholar (Imam Ahl al-Sunna — The Creed of the Majority) in Taha / Cairo, Egypt; Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari — philosopher (Founder of the Ash'ari school of kalam in Sunni Islam) in Basra.

title: Al-Ash'ari founds orthodox Sunni theology
description: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, a former Mu'tazili, develops a middle path between rationalism and literalism that becomes the dominant school of Sunni theological thought.

figures.name: Al-Ash'ari
figures.role: founder; created the dominant school of Sunni theology

outcome: Ash'ari theology became the mainstream of Sunni Islam, followed by the majority to this day

title: Deaths in 936 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 936 CE: Al-Ash'ari — scholar (Defender of Sunni Theology — Founder of Ash'ari Kalam) in Basra / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 940 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 940 CE: Abu Ishaq Shami — Sufi mystic (Syrian Mystic — Key Bridge in the Chishti Silsila) in Syria / Chisht, Afghanistan.

title: Al-Farabi writes The Virtuous City
description: Al-Farabi completes his masterwork on political philosophy, combining Plato's Republic with Islamic political thought to envision the ideal Islamic state.
sources: Al-Farabi, Kitab Ara Ahl al-Madina al-Fadila
outcome: Created Islamic political philosophy; influenced Ibn Sina, Maimonides, and Aquinas

title: Deaths in 941 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 941 CE: Al-Kulayni — scholar (Thiqa al-Islam — Greatest Shia Hadith Compiler) in Kulain (Rey) / Baghdad; Rudaki — poet (Father of Persian Poetry) in Rudak, Khorasan (Tajikistan).

title: Deaths in 943 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 943 CE: Sinan ibn Thabit — scientist (Physician who organised the first system of public hospitals in Baghdad) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 944 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 944 CE: Al-Maturidi — scholar (Imam al-Huda — Founder of Maturidi Kalam) in Samarkand, Uzbekistan; Abu Mansur al-Maturidi — philosopher (Philosopher of Samarkand) in Samarkand.

title: Deaths in 945 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 945 CE: Abu Bakr al-Shibli — Sufi mystic (Shaykh Baghdad — Companion of al-Hallaj) in Baghdad, Iraq; Al-Hamdani — scholar (Tongue of Yemen — Arab Geographer) in Sanaa, Yemen.

title: Deaths in 946 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 946 CE: Al-Suli — scholar (Greatest Chess Master of the Medieval Islamic World) in Baghdad, Iraq; Ibrahim ibn Sinan — scientist (Mathematician who advanced the geometry of conic sections and sundials) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 950 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 950 CE: Al-Farabi — philosopher (al-Mu'allim al-Thani — The Second Teacher) in Farab / Baghdad / Damascus.

title: Al-Mutanabbi — greatest Arabic poet — flourishes
description: Ahmad ibn al-Husayn al-Mutanabbi, universally regarded as the greatest Arabic poet, composes his masterworks at the court of Sayf al-Dawla in Aleppo.

figures.name: Al-Mutanabbi
figures.role: poet; composed at Sayf al-Dawla's court in Aleppo

outcome: Considered the greatest Arabic poet; his verse is still memorised and quoted across the Arab world

title: Al-Mas'udi writes Meadows of Gold
description: Ali al-Mas'udi completes Muruj al-Dhahab (Meadows of Gold), an encyclopedic work combining history, geography, and natural science — he is later called the 'Herodotus of the Arabs'.
outcome: Created a model of universal history combining science, geography, and culture

title: Deaths in 956 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 956 CE: Al-Masudi — historian (Herodotus of the Arabs) in Baghdad / Cairo / Fustat.

title: Deaths in 957 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 957 CE: Al-Istakhri — scholar (Author of the Book of Climates) in Persepolis / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 960 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 960 CE: Ahmad ibn Fadlan — scholar (Abbasid traveler who recorded the Vikings on the Volga) in Baghdad.

title: Conversion of the Karakhanids — Islam enters the Turkic steppe
description: The Karakhanid ruler Satuq Bughra Khan converts to Islam, bringing the first major Turkic dynasty into the Islamic world and opening Central Asia to Turkish-Islamic civilisation.
outcome: First mass Turkic conversion to Islam; opened the path for the Seljuks, Ottomans, and Mughals

title: Deaths in 965 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 965 CE: Al-Mutanabbi — poet (Greatest Arab Poet of the Classical Era) in Kufa / Aleppo / Shiraz.

title: Deaths in 967 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 967 CE: Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani — scholar (Author of the Book of Songs — Greatest Arabic Literary Anthology) in Isfahan / Aleppo / Baghdad; Sayf al-Dawla — ruler (Patron of al-Mutanabbi — Hamdanid Prince of Aleppo) in Aleppo, Syria.

title: Deaths in 968 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 968 CE: Abu Firas al-Hamdani — poet (Hamdanid prince-poet and author of the Rumiyyat prison poems) in Mosul.

title: Fatimids conquer Egypt and found Cairo
description: The Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli conquers Egypt and founds the new city of al-Qahira (Cairo) as the Fatimid capital, shifting the dynasty's power base from Tunisia to the Nile.
outcome: Founded Cairo — one of the greatest cities in Islamic history; Egypt became the Fatimid heartland

title: Al-Azhar Mosque founded in Cairo
description: The Fatimids found al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, which becomes al-Azhar University — the oldest degree-granting university in the world and the foremost centre of Sunni learning to this day.
outcome: Founded the institution that remains the world's foremost centre of Islamic scholarship

title: Al-Azhar becomes a university
description: Al-Azhar transitions from a mosque to a full university with regular courses in Islamic law, theology, logic, grammar, and the sciences — the oldest degree-granting university in the world.
sources: Dodge, Al-Azhar: A Millennium of Muslim Learning
outcome: World's oldest degree-granting university; remains the supreme authority in Sunni scholarship

title: Deaths in 974 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 974 CE: Al-Qadi al-Nu'man — scholar (Chief jurist of the Fatimid Caliphate and founder of Ismaili law) in Cairo.

title: Al-Aziz billah expands Fatimid empire
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Aziz expands the empire to include Syria and the Hijaz, making the Fatimids the most powerful Muslim dynasty controlling Mecca, Medina, Jerusalem, and Cairo.
outcome: Fatimids at their peak: controlled the three holiest cities of Islam simultaneously

title: Deaths in 979 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 979 CE: Ibn al-Jazzar — scientist (Tunisian physician whose Viaticum became a standard European medical text) in Kairouan.

title: Ibn Sina born — greatest Islamic polymath
description: Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna) is born near Bukhara; he will write the Canon of Medicine and the Book of Healing, shaping both Islamic and European science for centuries.

figures.name: Ibn Sina
figures.role: born; future author of the Canon of Medicine

outcome: His Canon was the standard medical textbook in Europe until the 17th century

title: Deaths in 982 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 982 CE: Ibn Khafif — Sufi mystic (Shaykh al-Kabir — The Great Shaykh of Shiraz) in Shiraz, Iran.

title: Deaths in 984 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 984 CE: Lubna of Cordoba — scholar (Mathematician, scribe, and librarian of the Cordoban caliphal court) in Cordoba.

title: Deaths in 985 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 985 CE: Al-Sijistani — philosopher (Philosopher of Baghdad — Custodian of Philosophy) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Al-Muqaddasi writes the best geographical survey
description: Muhammad al-Muqaddasi completes Ahsan al-Taqasim, the finest medieval geographical work, based on his personal travels across every region of the Islamic world.

figures.name: Al-Muqaddasi
figures.role: author; personally traveled every region he described

sources: Al-Muqaddasi, Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim
outcome: Created the most accurate and detailed geography of the medieval Islamic world

title: Deaths in 986 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 986 CE: Al-Sufi — scientist (Azophi — First to Describe the Andromeda Galaxy) in Isfahan / Shiraz, Iran.

title: Deaths in 987 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 987 CE: Abu Sulayman Sijistani — philosopher (Philosopher of Baghdad) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 988 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 988 CE: Abu Nasr al-Sarraj — scholar (Imam Ahl al-Tasawwuf) in Tus, Iran; Al-Sarraj al-Tusi — scholar (First Encyclopaedist of Sufism) in Tus, Khorasan; Ibn Hawqal — scholar (Traveller-Geographer — Surat al-Ard) in Nisibis / Cordoba / Cairo.

title: Deaths in 990 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 990 CE: Ibn al-Nadim — scholar (Author of the Fihrist — First Bio-Bibliographic Catalogue) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 991 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 991 CE: Al-Muqaddasi — scholar (Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions) in Jerusalem / Baghdad; Ibn Babawayh — scholar (Shaykh al-Saduq — Master of Shia Hadith) in Qum / Baghdad / Khorasan.

title: Deaths in 992 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 992 CE: Al-Amiri — philosopher (Islamic Philosopher on Happiness and Religion) in Nishapur / Baghdad / Bukhara; Jawhar al-Siqilli — ruler (Fatimid General — Founder of Cairo and al-Azhar) in Sicily / Cairo, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 994 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 994 CE: Haly Abbas — scientist (Persian physician and author of the Complete Book of Medical Art) in Shiraz.

title: Deaths in 996 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 996 CE: Abu Talib al-Makki — scholar (Qut al-Qulub — The Nourishment of Hearts) in Mecca / Baghdad.

title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah becomes Fatimid Caliph
description: The eccentric and controversial al-Hakim becomes Fatimid Caliph at age 11, beginning a reign marked by both brilliant patronage of science and erratic persecution.
outcome: His mysterious disappearance in 1021 gave rise to the Druze faith

title: Deaths in 998 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 998 CE: Abu al-Wafa Buzjani — scientist (Persian mathematician who advanced trigonometry and introduced the tangent function) in Baghdad.

title: Mahmud of Ghazna begins conquests into India
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazna launches the first of his seventeen campaigns into the Indian subcontinent, establishing Ghaznavid rule and bringing Islam deep into South Asia.
outcome: Began the systematic spread of Islam into the Indian subcontinent

title: Deaths in 999 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 999 CE: Sahl al-Su'luki — scholar (Mufti of Nishapur — Co-Mujaddid of the 4th Century) in Nishapur, Iran.

title: Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh
description: Abu al-Qasim Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), the Persian national epic of 50,000 couplets recounting Iran's pre-Islamic history — the longest poem by a single author.

figures.name: Firdowsi
figures.role: poet; completed the 50,000-couplet Persian national epic

outcome: Preserved Persian identity within Islam; the greatest literary work in the Persian language

title: Deaths in 1000 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1000 CE: Abu Bakr al-Kalabadhi — scholar (Systematizer of Early Sufi Doctrine) in Bukhara, Transoxiana; Abu Sahl al-Quhi — scientist (Persian mathematician who advanced conic sections and mathematical centres of gravity) in Baghdad; Abu-Mahmud Khojandi — scientist (Persian astronomer who built the great mural sextant of Rayy) in Khujand; Abu Dulaf al-Khazraji — traveler (Abbasid-era traveler who wrote two Risalas on Central Asia and China) in Aleppo.

title: Al-Hakim establishes Dar al-Ilm in Cairo
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim founds the Dar al-Ilm (House of Knowledge) in Cairo, a major library and academy rivalling Baghdad's House of Wisdom.
outcome: Created a rival intellectual centre to Baghdad under Fatimid patronage

title: Deaths in 1008 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1008 CE: Badi al-Zaman al-Hamadhani — scholar (Wonder of the Age — Inventor of the Maqama Literary Form) in Hamadan / Herat.

title: Deaths in 1009 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1009 CE: Ibn Yunus — scientist (Egyptian astronomer who compiled the great Hakimi Zij) in Cairo.

title: Al-Hakim orders destruction of Church of Holy Sepulchre
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim orders the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, an act later used as justification for the First Crusade.
outcome: Provided one of the pretexts for the Crusades nearly a century later

title: Deaths in 1010 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1010 CE: Ali ibn Isa al-Kahhal — scientist (Greatest ophthalmologist of the medieval Islamic world) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1012 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1012 CE: Al-Qabisi — scholar (Imam al-Maliki of Qayrawan) in Qayrawan, Tunisia.

title: Deaths in 1013 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1013 CE: Al-Baqillani — scholar (Sword of the Sunna — Master of Asharite Kalam) in Basra / Baghdad; Al-Zahrawi — scientist (Abulcasis — Father of Modern Surgery) in Medina al-Zahra, Cordoba, Spain.

title: Deaths in 1016 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1016 CE: Sharif al-Radi — poet (Poet of the Hashimites) in Baghdad, Iraq; Abu Hamid al-Isfarayini — scholar (Shaykh al-Shafi'iyya — Head of the Shafi'is in Baghdad) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 1020 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1020 CE: Firdowsi — poet (Homer of the East — Author of the Shahnameh) in Tus, Khorasan (Iran).

title: Ibn Sina completes the Canon of Medicine
description: Ibn Sina (Avicenna) completes al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, a million-word medical encyclopedia that will remain the standard textbook in both Islamic and European universities until the 17th century.

figures.name: Ibn Sina
figures.role: author; wrote the most influential medical text in history

outcome: Standard medical textbook worldwide for 600 years; translated into Latin, Hebrew, and Chinese

title: Deaths in 1021 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1021 CE: Al-Sulami of Nishapur — scholar (Imam al-Tasawwuf — Master of the Sufi Sciences) in Nishapur, Khorasan.

title: Disappearance of Caliph al-Hakim — Druze faith emerges
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim vanishes during a nighttime walk in the Muqattam hills; his followers believe he went into occultation, giving rise to the Druze faith.
outcome: Created the Druze religion; one of the most mysterious events in Islamic history

title: Deaths in 1022 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1022 CE: Al-Mufid — scholar (Shaykh al-Mufid — Architect of Shia Kalam) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 1023 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1023 CE: Al-Tawhidi — philosopher (Philosopher of the Litterateurs — Most Difficult of Writers) in Baghdad / Shiraz; Abū Hayyān al-Tawhīdī — philosopher (Philosopher of Iraq) in Iraq.

title: Deaths in 1025 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1025 CE: Abd al-Jabbar ibn Ahmad — philosopher (Philosopher of Hamadan) in Hamadan.

title: Deaths in 1029 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1029 CE: Ibn Hindu — scientist (Philosopher of Qom) in Qom; Kushyar Gilani — scientist (Persian mathematician and astronomer who influenced al-Biruni) in Gilan.

title: Deaths in 1030 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1030 CE: Mahmud of Ghazni — ruler (Sultan al-Ghazi — The Warrior Sultan) in Ghazni, Afghanistan; Miskawayh — philosopher (Father of Islamic Ethics) in Isfahan / Baghdad.

title: Al-Biruni writes Kitab al-Hind
description: Abu Rayhan al-Biruni completes his masterwork on Indian civilisation — the first objective scholarly study of another culture, covering religion, philosophy, science, and social customs.

figures.name: Al-Biruni
figures.role: author; wrote the first objective cross-cultural study in history

outcome: Founded comparative cultural studies; remains invaluable for understanding medieval India

title: Deaths in 1033 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1033 CE: Abu al-Hasan Kharaqani — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-Arifin of Khorasan — Bridge of the Naqshbandi Chain) in Kharaqan, Khorasan.

title: Deaths in 1036 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1036 CE: Abu Nasr Mansur — scientist (Persian mathematician and collaborator of al-Biruni) in Khwarazm.

title: Deaths in 1037 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1037 CE: Baba Kuhi of Shiraz — poet (Patron Saint of Shiraz) in Shiraz, Iran; Ibn Sina — philosopher (al-Shaykh al-Ra'is — Prince of Physicians) in Bukhara / Hamadan / Isfahan; Farrokhi Sistani — poet (Court poet of the Ghaznavids and master of the qasida) in Ghazni; Abu Mansur al-Baghdadi — scientist (Arab mathematician and theologian who contributed to number theory) in Baghdad.

title: Death of Ibn Sina
description: Ibn Sina dies in Hamadan aged 57; his Canon of Medicine and Book of Healing will dominate Islamic and European science for centuries.
outcome: Left the most influential medical and philosophical works in Islamic history

title: Deaths in 1038 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1038 CE: Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani — scholar (Author of Hilyat al-Awliya, major biographical dictionary of saints) in Isfahan.

title: Deaths in 1040 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1040 CE: Ibn al-Haytham — scientist (Father of Optics — Founder of the Scientific Method) in Basra / Cairo.

title: Deaths in 1044 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1044 CE: Sharif al-Murtada — scholar (Major Shia theologian and literary scholar of Baghdad) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1048 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1048 CE: Al-Biruni — scientist (Father of Comparative Anthropology and Geology) in Khwarezm / Ghazni.

title: Death of Al-Biruni
description: Al-Biruni dies after a lifetime of groundbreaking work in astronomy, mathematics, geography, mineralogy, and comparative religion.

figures.name: Al-Biruni
figures.role: died; pioneering polymath and cross-cultural scholar

outcome: Left over 140 works spanning every scientific discipline

title: Deaths in 1049 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1049 CE: Abu Sa'id Abu al-Khayr — poet (Pir-i Khorasan — First Sufi Quatrain Poet) in Mayhana, Khorasan.

title: Deaths in 1054 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1054 CE: Fakhruddin Gurgani — poet (Author of Vis and Ramin, earliest Persian romance epic) in Gorgan.

title: Seljuk Turks capture Baghdad
description: Tughril Beg's Seljuk Turks capture Baghdad, replacing the Shia Buyids as protectors of the Sunni Abbasid Caliph and establishing Sunni Turkish dominance across the eastern Islamic world.
outcome: Began the era of Turkish Sunni dominance; Seljuks became the most powerful Muslim dynasty

title: Deaths in 1058 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1058 CE: Al-Mawardi — scholar (Greatest Political Theorist of Medieval Islam) in Basra / Baghdad.

title: Al-Mawardi writes The Ordinances of Government
description: Al-Mawardi completes al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyyah, the foundational text of Islamic constitutional law defining the rights and duties of the Caliph.

figures.name: Al-Mawardi
figures.role: author; wrote the foundational text of Islamic constitutional law

outcome: Defined Islamic political theory for all subsequent centuries

title: Deaths in 1064 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1064 CE: Ibn Hazm — scholar (al-Andalusi — Polymath of Córdoba) in Córdoba / Jativa, al-Andalus.

title: Nizam al-Mulk founds the Nizamiyya madrasas
description: The Seljuk vizier Nizam al-Mulk establishes the Nizamiyya network of madrasas across the Islamic world, with the Baghdad Nizamiyya becoming the most prestigious university of its era.

figures.name: Nizam al-Mulk
figures.role: founder; created the madrasa system that educated the Islamic world

outcome: Created the institutional model of Islamic higher education that spread globally

title: Deaths in 1071 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1071 CE: Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi — scholar (Greatest historian of Baghdad and pioneer of hadith methodology) in Baghdad.

title: Battle of Manzikert — Seljuks defeat Byzantium
description: Sultan Alp Arslan's Seljuk army captures Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV at Manzikert, opening Anatolia to Turkish settlement and beginning the transformation of Asia Minor into Turkey.
outcome: Opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement; led directly to the Ottoman Empire

title: Deaths in 1072 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1072 CE: Al-Qushayri — scholar (Defender of Orthodox Sufism) in Nishapur, Iran; Ali al-Hujwiri — scholar (Data Ganj Bakhsh — Giver of Spiritual Treasures) in Ghazni / Lahore; Alp Arslan — ruler (Great Lion — Victor of Manzikert) in Marv / Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1078 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1078 CE: Mu'ayyad fi'l-Din al-Shirazi — scholar (Islamic Philosophy Scholar of Shiraz) in Shiraz.

title: Deaths in 1085 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1085 CE: Imam al-Juwayni — scholar (Imam al-Haramayn — Teacher of Al-Ghazali) in Nishapur / Mecca / Medina.

title: Toledo falls to Christian Reconquista
description: Alfonso VI of Castile captures Toledo, the former Visigothic capital, beginning the major phase of the Christian Reconquista and opening Arabic libraries to European scholars.
outcome: Toledo's Arabic libraries sparked the Latin translation movement that fuelled the European Renaissance

title: Deaths in 1087 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1087 CE: Al-Zarqali — scientist (Arzachel — Most Influential Astronomer of the 11th Century) in Toledo / Cordoba, Spain.

title: Deaths in 1088 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1088 CE: Nasir Khusraw — philosopher (Hujjat al-Khorasan — Ismaili Philosopher-Poet) in Khorasan / Cairo / Badakhshan.

title: Deaths in 1089 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1089 CE: Khwaja Abdullah Ansari — Sufi mystic (Pir-i Herat — Elder of Herat) in Herat, Afghanistan.

title: Deaths in 1090 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1090 CE: Al-Sarakhsi — scholar (Sun of the Imams — Greatest Hanafi Jurist) in Sarakhs / Fergana, Central Asia.

title: Al-Ghazali begins his spiritual crisis
description: Abu Hamid al-Ghazali, the most famous professor at Baghdad's Nizamiyya, suffers a spiritual crisis, abandons his prestigious career, and embarks on a decade of wandering and Sufi practice.

figures.name: Al-Ghazali
figures.role: abandoned his career at its peak to seek spiritual truth

outcome: His crisis and return produced the Ihya — the most influential work in Islamic history after the Quran

title: Deaths in 1091 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1091 CE: Wallada bint al-Mustakfi — poet (Andalusian princess-poet and literary salon hostess) in Cordoba.

title: Deaths in 1092 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1092 CE: Nizam al-Mulk — ruler (Khwaja Buzurg — The Great Vizier) in Tus / Baghdad, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1094 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1094 CE: Al-Bakri — scholar (Greatest Andalusian Geographer) in Cordoba / Seville, Spain.

title: Pope Urban II calls the First Crusade
description: Pope Urban II calls Christian Europe to arms at the Council of Clermont, launching the First Crusade to recapture Jerusalem from Muslim control.
outcome: Began two centuries of Crusader warfare that reshaped the entire Mediterranean world

title: Crusaders capture Jerusalem
description: The First Crusade captures Jerusalem after a bloody siege, massacring the Muslim and Jewish inhabitants and establishing the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem.
outcome: Shocked the Muslim world; Jerusalem lost to Christendom for 88 years until Saladin

title: Deaths in 1101 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1101 CE: Mahmud al-Kashgari — scholar (Father of Turkic Linguistics — Diwan Lughat al-Turk) in Kashgar, Central Asia.

title: Deaths in 1106 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1106 CE: Yusuf ibn Tashfin — ruler (Amir al-Muslimin — Commander of the Muslims) in Marrakesh, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1111 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1111 CE: Al-Ghazali — scholar (Hujjat al-Islam — Proof of Islam) in Tus / Baghdad.

title: Death of Al-Ghazali
description: Al-Ghazali dies in Tus after completing the Ihya Ulum al-Din, which reconciles Sunni orthodoxy with Sufism and reshapes Islamic thought permanently.

figures.name: Al-Ghazali
figures.role: died; author of the Ihya, the most influential Islamic book after the Quran

outcome: His Ihya reconciled orthodoxy with mysticism; shaped all subsequent Sunni Islam

title: Deaths in 1122 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1122 CE: Al-Baghawi — scholar (Muhyi al-Sunna — Reviver of the Sunnah) in Bagh, Khorasan, Iran; Al-Hariri of Basra — poet (Author of the Maqamat, masterpiece of Arabic prose literature) in Basra.

title: Omar Khayyam dies — poet, mathematician, astronomer
description: Omar Khayyam dies in Nishapur; his calendar reform is more accurate than the Gregorian, his algebra advanced cubic equations, and his Rubaiyat became the most translated Persian poem.
outcome: His calendar was more accurate than the Gregorian; his poetry became world-famous through FitzGerald

title: Deaths in 1123 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1123 CE: Ahmad Ghazali — Sufi mystic (Imam al-Mahabba — Imam of Love) in Tus / Qazvin.

title: Deaths in 1124 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1124 CE: Hasan-i Sabbah — ruler (Old Man of the Mountain — Founder of the Nizari Ismaili State) in Qom / Alamut, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1125 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1125 CE: Sharaf al-Zaman al-Marwazi — scientist (Persian physician and zoologist who authored the Nature of Animals) in Merv.

title: Deaths in 1127 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1127 CE: Ibn al-Sīd al-Baṭalyawsī — philosopher (Philosopher of Badajoz) in Badajoz.

title: Imad al-Din Zangi begins Muslim counter-crusade
description: The Turkish atabeg Imad al-Din Zangi unifies Mosul and Aleppo, beginning the organised Muslim military response to the Crusader states.
outcome: Began the Muslim counter-crusade that Nur al-Din and Saladin would complete

title: Deaths in 1130 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1130 CE: Ibn Tumart — reformer (Al-Mahdi — The Almohad Prophet) in Sus, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1131 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1131 CE: Omar Khayyam — poet (Philosopher-Poet of the Rubaiyat) in Nishapur, Khorasan, Iran; Sana'i of Ghazna — poet (Hakim al-Ghayb — Father of Sufi Masnavi Poetry) in Ghazna, Afghanistan.

title: Deaths in 1134 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1134 CE: Abu al-Salt — scientist (Andalusian polymath and author of works on medicine, astronomy and music) in Denia.

title: Deaths in 1138 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1138 CE: Ibn Bajja — philosopher (Avempace — First Great Andalusian Philosopher) in Saragossa / Seville / Fez; Arwa al-Sulayhi — ruler (Longest-reigning queen of Yemen and Ismaili patron) in Jibla.

title: Deaths in 1140 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1140 CE: Yusuf al-Hamadani — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Aqtab — Teacher of Yesevi and Ghujdawani) in Hamadan / Merv.

title: Deaths in 1142 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1142 CE: Abu Hafs al-Nasafi — scholar (Central Asian Hanafi jurist and creed author) in Samarkand.

title: Deaths in 1144 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1144 CE: Al-Zamakhshari — philosopher (Philosopher of Zamakhshar) in Zamakhshar.

title: Zangi recaptures Edessa from Crusaders
description: Zangi captures the Crusader County of Edessa, the first Crusader state to fall, triggering the Second Crusade.
outcome: First major Muslim victory against Crusaders; triggered the Second Crusade

title: Deaths in 1148 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1148 CE: Abu Bakr ibn al-Arabi — scholar (Leading Andalusian Maliki jurist and student of al-Ghazali) in Seville.

title: Deaths in 1149 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1149 CE: Al-Qadi Iyad — scholar (Imam al-Maghrib — Author of Al-Shifa) in Ceuta / Granada / Marrakesh.

title: Deaths in 1153 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1153 CE: Al-Shahrastani — philosopher (Muhammad al-Shahrastānī) in Shahristan District.

title: Al-Idrisi creates the most accurate world map
description: The Sicilian-Arab geographer al-Idrisi completes the Tabula Rogeriana for the Norman King Roger II of Sicily, the most accurate world map of the medieval period.

figures.name: Al-Idrisi
figures.role: cartographer; created the most accurate medieval world map

outcome: Most accurate world map for three centuries; used by European explorers

title: Deaths in 1155 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1155 CE: Al-Khazini — scientist (Physicist who wrote the Book of the Balance of Wisdom on mechanics and hydrostatics) in Merv.

title: Deaths in 1160 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1160 CE: Ibn al-Qalanisi — scholar (Damascene chronicler of the First Crusade) in Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1162 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1162 CE: Ibn Zuhr — scientist (Avenzoar — Greatest Clinical Physician of Islamic Spain) in Seville, Spain.

title: Deaths in 1165 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1165 CE: Al-Idrisi — scholar (al-Sharif al-Idrisi — World's Greatest Cartographer) in Ceuta / Palermo, Sicily; Abu'l-Barakat al-Baghdadi — scientist (Philosopher and physicist who challenged Aristotelian mechanics) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1166 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1166 CE: Abdul Qadir al-Jilani — founder (Ghawth al-A'zam — The Greatest Intercessor) in Gilan / Baghdad; Ahmed Yesevi — founder (Pir-i Turkistan — Father of Turkish Sufism) in Sayram / Turkestan.

title: Deaths in 1167 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1167 CE: Al-Sam'ani — scholar (Author of al-Ansab, the great dictionary of Arabic genealogical surnames) in Merv.

title: Deaths in 1168 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1168 CE: Abu Najib Suhrawardi — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Suhrawardi Path — Uncle and Teacher of Shihab al-Din) in Baghdad.

title: Saladin overthrows the Fatimid Caliphate
description: Salah al-Din al-Ayyubi (Saladin) abolishes the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt, restoring Sunni Islam and founding the Ayyubid dynasty.

figures.name: Salahuddin Ayyubi
figures.role: overthrew the Fatimids; restored Sunni Islam in Egypt

outcome: Ended 260 years of Shia rule in Egypt; united Egypt and Syria against the Crusaders

title: Deaths in 1174 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1174 CE: Nur al-Din Zangi — ruler (Al-Malik al-Adil — The Just King) in Mosul / Aleppo / Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1176 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1176 CE: Ibn Asakir — historian (Hafiz al-Sham — Greatest Historian of Damascus) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1178 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1178 CE: Shuhda al-Katiba — scholar (Fakhr al-Nisa — Pride of Women) in Baghdad, Iraq.

title: Deaths in 1179 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1179 CE: Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani — Sufi mystic (Founder of Silent Dhikr in the Khwajagan Chain) in Ghujduwan, Uzbekistan.

title: Deaths in 1180 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1180 CE: Al-Samawal al-Maghribi — scientist (Jewish-born mathematician who advanced algebra before converting to Islam) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1182 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1182 CE: Ahmad al-Rifai — founder (Imam al-Ariffin — Founding Pole of the Rifaiyya) in Wasit, Iraq; Ahmed ar-Rifa'i — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Rifa'iyya Sufi order) in Basra.

title: Deaths in 1185 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1185 CE: Ibn Tufayl — philosopher (Abubacer — Author of the First Philosophical Novel) in Cadiz / Marrakesh.

title: Saladin recaptures Jerusalem — Battle of Hattin
description: Saladin crushes the Crusader army at Hattin and recaptures Jerusalem after 88 years of Crusader rule, treating the defeated with mercy that contrasted sharply with the Crusaders' 1099 massacre.

figures.name: Salahuddin Ayyubi
figures.role: commander; recaptured Jerusalem with famous mercy

outcome: Recaptured Jerusalem for Islam; Saladin's mercy became legendary worldwide

title: Deaths in 1188 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1188 CE: Usama ibn Munqidh — scholar (Crusader-era memoirist and author of Kitab al-I'tibar) in Shaizar.

title: Deaths in 1189 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1189 CE: Anvari — poet (Master of the Persian panegyric qasida) in Balkh.

title: Deaths in 1191 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1191 CE: Yahya Suhrawardi (Maqtul) — philosopher (Shaykh al-Ishraq — Master of Illumination) in Sohrevard / Aleppo.

title: Deaths in 1193 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1193 CE: Salahuddin Ayyubi — warrior (Al-Nasir — Defender of the Faith; Liberator of Jerusalem) in Tikrit / Cairo / Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1198 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1198 CE: Abu Madyan — Sufi mystic (Shu'ayb al-Andalusi — Patron Saint of Tlemcen) in Seville / Bejaia, Algeria; Ibn Rushd — philosopher (al-Mu'allim al-Thalith — The Great Commentator) in Córdoba / Marrakesh.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Ibn Rushd dies in Marrakesh; his commentaries on Aristotle will ignite the intellectual revolution in medieval Europe that leads to Aquinas and the Renaissance.

figures.name: Ibn Rushd
figures.role: died; his Aristotle commentaries transformed European philosophy

outcome: His work reached Europe as 'Averroes' and sparked the Scholastic revolution

title: Deaths in 1201 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1201 CE: Ibn al-Jawzi — scholar (Imam al-Wa'iz — The Great Preacher of Baghdad) in Baghdad, Iraq; Abu'l-Faraj ibn al-Jawzi — philosopher (Ibn al-Jawzi (c) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1204 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1204 CE: Abu al-Abbas as-Sabti — Sufi mystic (Sidi Bel Abbas) in Ceuta under Muslim rule.

title: Deaths in 1206 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1206 CE: al-Jazari — scientist (Father of Robotics and Mechanical Engineering) in Upper Mesopotamia / Diyarbakir, Turkey; Muhammad of Ghor — ruler (Ghurid sultan who established Muslim rule in northern India) in Ghazni.

title: Delhi Sultanate founded
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, a former Ghurid slave-general, establishes the Delhi Sultanate, beginning over three centuries of Muslim rule in northern India.
outcome: Began 320 years of Muslim rule in India; paved the way for the Mughal Empire

title: Genghis Khan unifies the Mongol tribes
description: Temüjin is proclaimed Genghis Khan (Universal Ruler), unifying the Mongol tribes into the military force that will devastate the Islamic world.
outcome: Created the military machine that would destroy Baghdad and reshape the Islamic world

title: Deaths in 1207 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1207 CE: Usman Harooni — Sufi mystic (Chishti Sufi master and teacher of Moinuddin Chishti) in Nishapur.

title: Deaths in 1209 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1209 CE: Fakhr al-Din Razi — scholar (Ibn al-Khatib — The Great Encyclopaedist) in Ray / Khorasan / Herat; Nizami Ganjavi — poet (Hakim Nizami — Master of the Persian Romantic Epic) in Ganja, Azerbaijan; Ruzbihan Baqli — Sufi mystic (Shaykh-i Shirin — Master of Intoxicated Love) in Shiraz, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1217 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1217 CE: Ibn Jubayr — historian (The Andalusian Pilgrim) in Valencia / Granada, Andalusia.

title: Mongols destroy Bukhara and Samarkand
description: Genghis Khan's armies sack the great Central Asian cities of Bukhara and Samarkand, destroying centuries of Islamic civilisation in Transoxiana.
outcome: Destroyed the cultural heartland of eastern Islam; millions killed

title: Deaths in 1221 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1221 CE: Farid ud-Din Attar — poet (Attar — Imam of the Sufi Poets) in Nishapur, Iran; Najm al-Din Kubra — founder (Wali Tarash — Manufacturer of Saints) in Khiva / Khwarezm.

title: Deaths in 1223 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1223 CE: Ibn Qudama al-Maqdisi — scholar (Muwaffaq al-Din — The Hanbali Master) in Qudama / Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1227 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1227 CE: Abd al-Salam ibn Mashish — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Wujud — Teacher of Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili) in Jabal al-Alam, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1228 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1228 CE: Ibn Mashish — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Wujud — Spiritual Father of al-Shadhili) in Jebel Alam, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1229 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1229 CE: Yaqut al-Hamawi — scholar (Author of the Geographical Dictionary) in Hamah / Baghdad / Mosul.

title: Al-Kamil cedes Jerusalem to Frederick II by treaty
description: The Ayyubid Sultan al-Kamil negotiates the peaceful return of Jerusalem to Frederick II's Crusaders for ten years, shocking both Muslim and Christian worlds.
outcome: Jerusalem changed hands by diplomacy not war; showed pragmatism could coexist with faith

title: Deaths in 1230 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1230 CE: Moinuddin Chishti — founder (Gharib Nawaz — Benefactor of the Poor) in Sistan / Ajmer, India.

title: Deaths in 1231 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1231 CE: Baha ud-Din Walad — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-Ulama — Father of Rumi) in Balkh / Konya, Anatolia; Abd al-Latif al-Baghdadi — scientist (Physician and polymath who wrote a pioneering account of Egypt) in Baghdad.

title: Deaths in 1233 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1233 CE: Ibn al-Athir — scholar (Greatest Universal Historian of the Islamic Middle Ages) in Jazira / Mosul / Damascus; Sayf al-Din al-Amidi — scholar (Major Ash'ari theologian and author of Abkar al-Afkar) in Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1234 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1234 CE: Shihab al-Din al-Suhrawardi — founder (Shaykh al-Shuyukh — Founder of Suhrawardiyya) in Sohrevard / Baghdad; Abu Hafs Umar al-Suhrawardi — Sufi mystic (Philosopher of Sohrevard) in Sohrevard; Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad — scholar (Biographer of Saladin and qadi of Aleppo) in Aleppo.

title: Deaths in 1235 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1235 CE: Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Delhi — Pole of Delhi) in Osh / Delhi, India; Umar Ibn al-Farid — poet (Sultan al-'Ashiqin — Sultan of Lovers) in Cairo / Mecca, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1238 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1238 CE: Auhad al-Din Kirmani — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Aqtab of Kirman) in Kirman / Baghdad, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1240 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1240 CE: Ibn Arabi — philosopher (Shaykh al-Akbar — The Greatest Master) in Murcia / Damascus.

title: Death of Ibn Arabi — greatest Sufi metaphysician
description: Muhyi al-Din ibn Arabi dies in Damascus; his Fusus al-Hikam and al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya constitute the most comprehensive mystical philosophy in Islam.

figures.name: Ibn Arabi
figures.role: died; 'Greatest Master' of Islamic mystical philosophy

outcome: His 'Unity of Being' (wahdat al-wujud) became the dominant framework of Islamic mysticism

title: Deaths in 1244 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1244 CE: Jalal al-Din Tabrizi — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Aqtab of Bengal — Pioneer of Islam in Eastern India) in Tabriz / Bengal, India.

title: Deaths in 1246 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1246 CE: Qazi Hamiduddin Nagori — Sufi mystic (Sufi of the Mill) in Nagaur, Rajasthan, India.

title: Deaths in 1248 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1248 CE: Ibn al-Baitar — scientist (Greatest Botanist and Pharmacologist of the Middle Ages) in Malaga, Spain / Damascus; Shams-i-Tabrizi — Sufi mystic (Shams al-Din — Spiritual Master of Rumi) in Tabriz / Konya.

title: Deaths in 1255 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1255 CE: Sunjata Keïta — ruler (Lion King — Founder of the Mali Empire) in Niani, Mali Empire (modern Guinea).

title: Deaths in 1256 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1256 CE: Najm al-Din Razi — Sufi mystic (Kubrawiyya Mystic of Ray) in Ray; Sibt ibn al-Jawzi — scholar (Grandson of Ibn al-Jawzi and Hanafi historian of Damascus) in Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1258 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1258 CE: Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili — founder (Qutb al-Aqtab — Pole of Poles) in Morocco / Egypt.

title: Mongols destroy Baghdad — fall of the Abbasid Caliphate
description: Hulagu Khan's Mongol army sacks Baghdad, kills Caliph al-Musta'sim, and destroys the House of Wisdom, ending 508 years of Abbasid rule in the most devastating event in Islamic history.
outcome: End of the Abbasid Caliphate; books of the House of Wisdom thrown into the Tigris

title: Battle of Ain Jalut — Mamluks halt the Mongols
description: The Egyptian Mamluk army under Qutuz and Baybars defeats the Mongols at Ain Jalut in Palestine, marking the first major Mongol defeat and saving Egypt, the Hijaz, and North Africa from invasion.

figures.name: Baybars
figures.role: Mamluk commander; led the decisive charge

outcome: First major Mongol defeat; saved the western Islamic world from destruction

title: Deaths in 1261 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1261 CE: al-Mustansir II (Cairo) — caliph (1st Cairo Shadow Abbasid Caliph) in Cairo, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1265 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1265 CE: Baba Farid — Sufi mystic (Chishti Sufi saint and father of Punjabi poetry) in Pakpattan.

title: Deaths in 1266 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1266 CE: Fariduddin Ganjshakar — Sufi mystic (Baba Farid — Ganj Shakar, Treasure of Sugar) in Kothewal / Pakpattan, Pakistan.

title: Deaths in 1267 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1267 CE: Baha-ud-din Zakariya — Sufi mystic (Shaykh al-Islam of Multan) in Multan, Pakistan.

title: Deaths in 1269 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1269 CE: Al-Shushtari — poet (Shaykh al-Malamatiyya) in Guadix, Andalusia / Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1270 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1270 CE: Ibn Sab'in — philosopher (Shaykh al-Ishraq of the West) in Murcia, Andalusia.

title: Deaths in 1271 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1271 CE: Hajji Bektash Veli — founder (Sultan al-Awliya of Anatolia) in Nishapur / Anatolia.

title: Deaths in 1273 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1273 CE: Al-Qurtubi — scholar (Greatest Andalusian Quranic Exegete) in Cordoba, Spain / Munya Bani Khasib, Egypt; Jalal ad-Din Rumi — founder (Mawlana — Our Master; Sultan of Poets) in Balkh / Konya, Anatolia; Muhammad I of Granada — ruler (Ibn al-Ahmar — Founder of Nasrid Dynasty and the Alhambra) in Arjona / Granada, Al-Andalus.

title: Death of Jalal ad-Din Rumi
description: Rumi dies in Konya; his Masnavi (26,000 couplets) becomes the most widely read mystical poem in the Islamic world and eventually the best-selling poet in America.

figures.name: Jalal ad-Din Rumi
figures.role: died; author of the Masnavi, the greatest Sufi poem

outcome: The Masnavi became 'the Quran in Persian'; Rumi became the world's best-selling poet

title: Deaths in 1274 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1274 CE: Hamid al-Din Nagori — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-Tarikain — King of the Two Paths) in Nagaur, Rajasthan, India; Lal Shahbaz Qalandar — Sufi mystic (Jhule Lal — Red-Robed Falcon of Sehwan) in Marwand / Sehwan Sharif, Pakistan; Nasir al-Din al-Tusi — scientist (Khwaja Nasir — Master of the Wise) in Tus / Maragha, Iran; Sadr al-Din al-Qunawi — scholar (Khatm al-Awliya' — Seal of the Friends) in Konya, Anatolia.

title: Deaths in 1276 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1276 CE: Ahmad al-Badawi — founder (Sayyid al-Badawi — Greatest Saint of Egypt) in Fez / Tanta, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1277 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1277 CE: Baybars — ruler (Lion of Egypt — Destroyer of the Crusader and Mongol Threat) in Qipchaq Steppe / Cairo / Damascus; Yahya ibn Sharif al-Nawawi — scholar (Muhyi al-Din — Reviver of Religion) in Nawa / Damascus, Syria.

title: Death of Baybars — greatest Mamluk Sultan
description: Sultan Baybars dies after transforming the Mamluk state into the dominant power of the Islamic world, defeating both Crusaders and Mongols.

figures.name: Baybars
figures.role: died; defeated Crusaders and Mongols, built the Mamluk empire

outcome: Built the Mamluk military state that protected Islam for 250 years

title: Deaths in 1282 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1282 CE: Ibn Khallikan — historian (Wafayat al-A'yan — Master Biographer of Islam) in Erbil / Cairo / Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1283 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1283 CE: Zakariya al-Qazwini — scholar (Father of Islamic Geography — Aja'ib al-Makhluqat) in Qazvin, Iran; Siraj al-Din al-Urmawi — philosopher (Persian logician and author of Maṭaliʿ al-Anwar) in Urmia; Ata-Malik Juvayni — scholar (Persian historian and author of the History of the World-Conqueror) in Tabriz.

title: Deaths in 1286 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1286 CE: Al-Baydawi — philosopher (Naṣir ad-Din al-Bayḍawi) in Ardakan.

title: Deaths in 1287 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1287 CE: Abu al-Abbas al-Mursi — Sufi mystic (2nd Master of the Shadhiliyya — Imam of Alexandria) in Murcia / Alexandria, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1288 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1288 CE: Ibn al-Nafis — scientist (Discoverer of Pulmonary Circulation) in Damascus / Cairo; Al-Shahrazuri — philosopher (Philosopher).

title: Deaths in 1289 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1289 CE: Fakhr al-Din Iraqi — poet (Imam al-'Ashiqin — Imam of the Lovers) in Hamadan / Konya / Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1290 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1290 CE: Qalawun — ruler (Mamluk sultan who established a dynasty and built Cairo's great hospital) in Cairo.

title: Deaths in 1291 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1291 CE: Alauddin Sabir Kaliyari — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Chishti-Sabiri Branch — The Jalali Saint of Kalyar) in Kalyar, Uttarakhand, India; Saadi Shirazi — poet (Shaykh Sa'di — Master of Persian Moral Poetry) in Shiraz / global travels.

title: Fall of Acre — end of the Crusader states
description: The Mamluks capture Acre, the last major Crusader stronghold in the Holy Land, ending nearly two centuries of Crusader presence in the Levant.
outcome: End of the Crusades in the Holy Land after 192 years

title: Deaths in 1294 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1294 CE: Al-Busiri — poet (Imam al-Madih — Master of Prophetic Praise) in Busa / Alexandria, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1296 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1296 CE: Ibrahim al-Dusuqi — founder (Fourth Egyptian Pole — Qutb al-Aqtab) in Disuq, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1298 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1298 CE: Ibn Wasil — historian (Mufarrij al-Kurub — Primary Historian of the Crusades) in Hama, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1299 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1299 CE: Al-Bahrani — philosopher (Philosopher).

title: Osman I founds the Ottoman state
description: Osman I declares independence from the Seljuks of Rum, founding the Ottoman beylik in northwestern Anatolia that will grow into one of history's greatest empires.

figures.name: Osman I
figures.role: founder; established the Ottoman dynasty in Anatolia

outcome: Founded the dynasty that would rule for 624 years and conquer Constantinople

title: Deaths in 1301 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1301 CE: Zahed Gilani — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Zahediyya Sufi order and spiritual grandfather of the Safavids) in Lahijan.

title: Deaths in 1302 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1302 CE: Ibn Daqiq al-'Id — scholar (Taqi al-Din — Master of Legal Hadith) in Cairo, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1309 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1309 CE: Ibn Ata Allah al-Iskandari — scholar (Taj al-Din — 3rd Master of the Shadhiliyya) in Alexandria / Cairo, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1311 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1311 CE: Qutb al-Din Shirazi — scientist (Allama al-A'zam — The Greatest Scholar) in Shiraz / Tabriz / Sivas.

title: Deaths in 1312 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1312 CE: Sultan Walad — founder (Organiser of the Mawlawi Order) in Konya, Anatolia.

title: Deaths in 1318 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1318 CE: Kamal al-Din al-Farisi — scientist (Discoverer of the Correct Explanation of the Rainbow) in Tabriz, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1320 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1320 CE: Yunus Emre — poet (Hak Ashigi — Lover of God) in Anatolia.

title: Deaths in 1321 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1321 CE: Ibn al-Banna al-Marrakushi — scientist (Greatest Moroccan Mathematician) in Marrakesh, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1325 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1325 CE: Allama Hilli — scholar (Ayatollah al-Alam — Sign of God in the World) in Hilla, Iraq; Amir Khusrow — poet (Tuti-yi Hind — Parrot of India; Father of Urdu) in Etah / Delhi, India; Nizamuddin Auliya — Sufi mystic (Mahbub-e-Ilahi — Beloved of God) in Badaon / Delhi, India.

title: Ibn Battuta begins his 30-year journey
description: The 21-year-old Moroccan scholar Ibn Battuta leaves Tangier for hajj, beginning a 30-year journey covering 120,000 km across the entire Islamic world and beyond.
outcome: Traveled further than Marco Polo; his Rihla is the most detailed medieval travel account

title: Deaths in 1326 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1326 CE: Osman I — ruler (Founder of the Ottoman Empire) in Sogut / Bursa, Anatolia.

title: Ottomans capture Bursa — first capital
description: Orhan, son of Osman I, captures the major Byzantine city of Bursa, making it the first Ottoman capital and establishing the Ottomans as a major power.
outcome: First Ottoman capital; the dynasty had arrived as a major power

title: Deaths in 1328 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1328 CE: Ibn Taymiyya — reformer (Shaykh al-Islam — The Controversial Reformer) in Harran / Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1330 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1330 CE: Abd al-Razzaq al-Qashani — Sufi mystic (Sufi commentator and expositor of Ibn Arabi's thought) in Kashan.

title: Birth of Ibn Khaldun
description: Abd al-Rahman ibn Khaldun is born in Tunis; he will write the Muqaddimah, inventing the sciences of sociology, historiography, and economics.

figures.name: Ibn Khaldun
figures.role: born; future father of sociology and historiography

outcome: His Muqaddimah is considered the foundational text of social science

title: Deaths in 1333 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1333 CE: Al-Nuwayri — scholar (Mamluk encyclopedist and author of Nihayat al-Arab) in Cairo.

title: Deaths in 1335 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1335 CE: Shah Rukn-e-Alam — Sufi mystic (Rukn-e-Alam — Pillar of the World) in Multan, Pakistan.

title: Deaths in 1337 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1337 CE: Mansa Musa — ruler (Emperor of Mali whose pilgrimage to Mecca displayed legendary wealth) in Niani.

title: Deaths in 1339 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1339 CE: Zaynab bint al-Kamal — scholar (Musnida of Syria) in Damascus, Syria; Hamdallah Mustawfi — scholar (Persian historian and geographer of the Ilkhanid period) in Qazvin.

title: Deaths in 1340 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1340 CE: Mahmud Shabistari — poet (Imam of Persian Sufi Poetry) in Tabriz, Iran.

title: Black Death reaches the Islamic world
description: The bubonic plague reaches Alexandria and spreads across the Islamic world, killing roughly a third of the population of Egypt, Syria, and North Africa.
outcome: Devastated the Islamic world's population and economy; weakened the Mamluk state

title: Deaths in 1348 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1348 CE: Al-Dhahabi — historian (Imam al-Jarh wa'l-Ta'dil — Greatest Islamic Biographical Historian) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1349 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1349 CE: Ibn Fadl Allah al-Umari — scholar (Mamluk encyclopedist and author of Masalik al-Absar on the peoples of the world) in Damascus.

title: Deaths in 1350 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1350 CE: Dawud al-Kayseri — scholar (Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottomans) in Kayseri / Cairo / Bursa; Ibn al-Qayyim — scholar (Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya — Scholar of the Heart) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Ibn Battuta returns home after 30 years
description: Ibn Battuta returns to Morocco after visiting every Muslim country, China, Southeast Asia, Mali, and East Africa — the greatest traveler of the medieval world.

figures.name: Ibn Battuta
figures.role: returned; had visited every Muslim country on Earth

outcome: Most extensive pre-modern travel account; visited more of the world than any person before him

title: Deaths in 1355 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1355 CE: Al-Idji — scholar (Greatest Asharite Theologian of the 14th Century) in Ij, Shiraz, Iran; Adud al-Din al-Iji — scholar (Major Ash'ari theologian and author of al-Mawaqif) in Ij.

title: Deaths in 1356 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1356 CE: Nasiruddin Chiragh Dehlawi — Sufi mystic (Chiragh-e-Delhi — Lamp of Delhi) in Ayodhya / Delhi, India.

title: Deaths in 1363 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1363 CE: Al-Safadi — scholar (Greatest Medieval Arabic Biographer) in Safad (Palestine) / Damascus / Cairo.

title: Deaths in 1368 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1368 CE: Ibn Battuta — historian (Greatest Traveller of the Ancient World) in Tangier / Global.

title: Timur begins his conquests from Samarkand
description: Timur (Tamerlane) seizes power in Transoxiana and begins three decades of devastating conquest from Delhi to Damascus, rebuilding Samarkand as his magnificent capital.

figures.name: Timur
figures.role: conqueror; launched 30 years of campaigns from Samarkand

outcome: Devastated much of the Islamic world but made Samarkand the most splendid city on Earth

title: Deaths in 1370 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1370 CE: Al-Subki — scholar (Taj al-Din — Crown of Religion) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1373 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1373 CE: Ibn Kathir — scholar (Imam al-Tafsir — Master of Quranic Exegesis) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1375 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1375 CE: Ibn al-Shatir — scientist (Damascene astronomer whose planetary models anticipated Copernicus) in Damascus.

title: Ibn Khaldun writes the Muqaddimah
description: Ibn Khaldun completes the Muqaddimah (Introduction to History), inventing the science of civilisation (ilm al-umran) — effectively founding sociology, historiography, and economics in a single work.

figures.name: Ibn Khaldun
figures.role: author; wrote the founding text of social science

outcome: Invented sociology and scientific historiography centuries before any European equivalent

title: Deaths in 1380 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1380 CE: Amir Kulal — Sufi mystic (Direct Teacher of Baha-ud-Din Naqshband) in Sukhari / Bukhara, Uzbekistan.

title: Deaths in 1381 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1381 CE: Sharaf al-Din Maneri — scholar (Makhdumul Mulk — Master of the Kingdom) in Bihar, India.

title: Deaths in 1384 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1384 CE: Makhdum Jahaniyan — Sufi mystic (Jahangasht — World Traveller of Uch Sharif) in Uch Sharif, Pakistan; Mir Sayyid Ali Hamadani — Sufi mystic (Shah-i Hamadan — King of Hamadan) in Hamadan, Iran / Kashmir.

title: Deaths in 1385 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1385 CE: Sayyid Ali Hamadani — Sufi mystic (Kubrawiyya Sufi master who spread Islam in Kashmir) in Hamadan.

title: Deaths in 1388 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1388 CE: Abu Ishaq al-Shatibi — scholar (Maliki jurist who founded the theory of maqasid al-sharia) in Granada.

title: Deaths in 1389 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1389 CE: Baha-ud-Din Naqshband — founder (Shah Naqshband — Founder of Naqshbandiyya) in Bukhara, Uzbekistan; Murad I — ruler (Ottoman sultan who established the empire in the Balkans) in Bursa.

title: Battle of Kosovo — Ottoman expansion into the Balkans
description: The Ottomans defeat a Serbian-led coalition at the Battle of Kosovo, establishing Ottoman dominance in the Balkans despite Sultan Murad I being killed.
outcome: Opened the Balkans to Ottoman rule; became a defining myth for both Turkish and Serbian identity

title: Deaths in 1390 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1390 CE: Al-Taftazani — scholar (Shaykh al-Islam of the Timurid Era) in Taftazan, Khorasan / Samarkand; Hafez — poet (Lisan al-Ghayb — Tongue of the Unseen) in Shiraz, Iran; Ibn Abbad al-Rundi — Sufi mystic (Shaykh al-Zawiya of Fez) in Ronda / Fez, Morocco.

title: Deaths in 1393 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1393 CE: Ibn Rajab al-Hanbali — scholar (Hafiz Ibn Rajab — Imam of the Later Hanbalis) in Damascus, Syria.

title: Deaths in 1394 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1394 CE: Fazlallah Astarabadi — founder (Shaykh al-Hurufiyya — Founder of Letter Mysticism) in Astarabad / Tabriz / Baku / Nakhchivan.

title: Battle of Nicopolis — Crusade defeated
description: Sultan Bayezid I crushes a massive European Crusade at Nicopolis on the Danube, confirming Ottoman military supremacy over Christian Europe.
outcome: Last major medieval Crusade failed; Ottoman dominance in southeastern Europe confirmed

title: Timur sacks Delhi
description: Timur invades India and sacks Delhi, massacring the population and carrying off enormous plunder, devastating the Delhi Sultanate.

figures.name: Timur
figures.role: invader; sacked Delhi and massacred its population

outcome: Devastated northern India; weakened the Delhi Sultanate beyond recovery

title: Timur sacks Damascus and Baghdad
description: Timur sacks Damascus (deporting its craftsmen to Samarkand) and then Baghdad, piling pyramids of skulls outside its walls.

figures.name: Timur
figures.role: destroyer; sacked both Damascus and Baghdad

outcome: Devastated the two great Arab capitals; Damascus lost its famous sword-makers to Samarkand

title: Deaths in 1405 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1405 CE: Al-Damiri — scholar (Greatest Islamic Zoologist) in Cairo, Egypt; Ali ibn Wafa — Sufi mystic (Shaykh al-Shadhiliyya of Egypt) in Cairo, Egypt; Timur — ruler (Tamerlane — Conqueror of Eurasia) in Kesh (Shahrisabz), Uzbekistan.

title: Death of Timur
description: Timur dies at Otrar while preparing to invade China; his empire fragments but his descendants, the Timurids, create a golden age of art and architecture in Herat and Samarkand.

figures.name: Timur
figures.role: died; his Timurid successors created a cultural golden age

outcome: His death saved China; Timurid successors created the greatest era of Central Asian art

title: Deaths in 1406 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1406 CE: Ibn Khaldun — historian (Father of Sociology and Modern Historiography) in Tunis / Fez / Cairo.

title: Death of Ibn Khaldun in Cairo
description: Ibn Khaldun dies in Cairo while serving as Maliki chief judge; his Muqaddimah will be rediscovered centuries later as the founding work of social science.

figures.name: Ibn Khaldun
figures.role: died; father of sociology and scientific historiography

outcome: His work was centuries ahead of its time; now recognised as the founder of social science

title: Deaths in 1413 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1413 CE: Al-Jurjani — scholar (al-Sayyid al-Sharif — Master of Islamic Definitions) in Gurgan / Shiraz / Samarkand; Ali ibn Mohammed al-Jurjani — philosopher (Philosopher of Gorgan) in Gorgan.

title: Deaths in 1418 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1418 CE: Ahmad al-Qalqashandi — scientist (Mamluk encyclopedist and author of Subh al-A'sha on chancery practice) in Cairo.

title: Zheng He's fleet visits Mecca and East Africa
description: The Chinese Muslim admiral Zheng He commands treasure fleets that reach Mecca, Hormuz, and the East African coast, demonstrating Chinese maritime power across the Indian Ocean.

figures.name: Zheng He
figures.role: Chinese Muslim admiral; commanded the largest fleet in history

outcome: Largest pre-modern naval expeditions; a Muslim admiral bridging Chinese and Islamic worlds

title: Deaths in 1422 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1422 CE: Gesu Daraz — Sufi mystic (Bande Nawaz — Benefactor of Servants) in Delhi / Gulbarga (Deccan), India.

title: Deaths in 1429 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1429 CE: Al-Kashi — scientist (Greatest Mathematician of the 15th Century — Pi to 16 Decimal Places) in Kashan / Samarkand; Suleyman Celebi — poet (Author of the Mevlid, the most beloved Turkish devotional poem) in Bursa.

title: Deaths in 1431 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1431 CE: Molla Fenari — scholar (First Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottoman Empire) in Bursa, Ottoman Empire; Shah Ni'matullah Wali — founder (Sultan al-Awliya — King of the Saints) in Aleppo / Kirman, Iran.

title: Deaths in 1435 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1435 CE: Zheng He — scholar (San Bao — Admiral of the Seven Voyages) in Yunnan, China / Indian Ocean.

title: Deaths in 1442 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1442 CE: Al-Maqrizi — scholar (Greatest Historian of Mamluk Egypt) in Cairo, Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1444 CE
